title: Centrally Built-in Power-Terminal Matrix Transformer for Vertical Power Delivery in LLC DCX
authors: Tongrui Sun, Wenjie Chen, Panming Li, Wei Zhou, Jiarun Xie, Xingwei Huang, Kangping Wang, Xu Yang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: In low-voltage, high-current LLC-DCX converters, vertical power delivery (VPD) tightly couples power terminal placement with transformer geometry, constraining achievable current density and scalability. This paper proposes a centrally built-in power-terminal (CBiPT) matrix transformer, in which the power terminals are embedded within the transformer to reduce external PCB power routing. The proposed structure supports both horizontal and vertical matrix extension, enabling high turns ratio and high current density while maintaining a compact footprint. The proposed structure is designed, optimized, and thermally analyzed, based on which a 48 V–0.75 V LLC-DCX prototype is built. The prototype achieves a peak efficiency of 94.2%. Under immersion liquid cooling, it delivers up to 800 A at full load, corresponding to a current density of 1.12 A/mm².

title: Spatiotemporal Satellite User Resource Forecasting in MEO–LEO Networks via a CNN–LSTM Model
authors: Yi Jing, Chunxiao Jiang, Jiawei Wang, Jiachen Sun, Sicong Zhou, Yafeng Zhan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Large-scale low Earth orbit (LEO) constellations are driving satellite communications toward increasingly dynamic and user-centric operation, making short-horizon demand forecasting important for proactive resource reservation and scheduling in cooperative MEO–LEO networks. To support this task, this paper constructs a satellite user request-map dataset from desensitized operational business records by transforming raw logs into grid-structured spatiotemporal demand maps through temporal slicing and spatial aggregation. Based on a cross-layer MEO–LEO workflow, we further develop a CNN–LSTM hybrid predictor that captures spatial patterns within each demand snapshot and temporal dependencies across historical windows, so as to support short-horizon forecasting of future demand maps. Experiments on 5-minute demand maps with a 30-minute forecasting horizon show that the proposed method achieves 90.59% mean total-traffic accuracy, outperforming Heuristic, ConvLSTM, and Transformer-Latent by 3.02, 3.43, and 3.66 percentage points, respectively. These results indicate that the proposed scheme can effectively characterize the spatiotemporal regularities of satellite service traffic and provide practical support for proactive reservation and scheduling in cooperative MEO–LEO systems.

title: Near-Optimal Reinforcement Learning With Shuffle Differential Privacy
authors: Shaojie Bai, Mohammad Sadegh Talebi, Chengcheng Zhao, Peng Cheng, Jiming Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a powerful tool for sequential decision-making, but its application is often hindered by privacy concerns arising from its sensitive interaction data. This challenge is particularly acute in advanced networked systems, where learning from operational and user data can expose systems to privacy inference attacks. Existing differential privacy (DP) models for RL are often inadequate: the centralized model requires a fully trusted server, creating a single point of failure risk, while the local model incurs significant performance degradation that renders it unsuitable for many networked applications. This paper addresses this gap by leveraging the emerging shuffle model of privacy, an intermediate trust model that provides strong privacy guarantees without a centralized trust assumption. We present Shuffle Differentially Private Policy Elimination (SDP-PE), the first generic policy elimination-based algorithm for episodic RL under the shuffle model. Our method introduces a novel exponential batching schedule and a “forgetting” mechanism to balance the competing demands of privacy and learning performance. Our analysis shows that SDP-PE achieves a near-optimal regret bound, demonstrating a superior privacy-regret trade-off with utility comparable to the centralized model while significantly outperforming the local model. The numerical experiments also corroborate our theoretical results and demonstrate the effectiveness of SDP-PE. This work establishes the viability of the shuffle model for secure data-driven decision-making in networked systems.

title: Sustainable Federated Learning in 6G Networks: The Case for Cybersecurity
authors: Gokcan Cantali, Wissem Soussi, Gurkan Gur, Burkhard Stiller
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: 6G networks are expected to integrate distributed artificial intelligence methods, such as federated learning (FL), across layers from core infrastructure to end-user applications. While FL offers significant advantages by enabling collaborative model training without sharing local and sensitive data, its deployment in large-scale, complex 6G environments raises important sustainability and security concerns. In particular, FL's communication overhead, including carbon footprint, requires careful optimization to ensure sustainable operation. Simultaneously, the cybersecurity implications of sustainable FL remain underexplored. In this work, we examine the sustainability challenges associated with FL and analyze them from a cybersecurity perspective through a use case, highlighting relevant trade-offs and key research directions for future 6G networks.

title: Barrier-Engineered Drain-Adaptive Thyristor 1T-DRAM for Disturb Suppression
authors: Hyeon-Seo Do, Chae-Ryang Heo, Sung-Woong Chung
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: Capacitorless 1T-DRAM is attractive for high-density memory, but its implementation is limited by the write/disturb trade-off, sneak current, and array-level IR-drop/RC delay. Here, we propose a barrier-engineered drain-adaptive thyristor 1T-DRAM in a 4F² cell. An array configuration utilizing a ground source plate is proposed to suppress IR drop. More importantly, the adaptive barrier gate dynamically modulates the internal barrier, lowering it for write/read operations while maintaining a high barrier in disturb/sneak states. The proposed operation is explained from a device-physics perspective in terms of capacitive coupling, barrier-pinning, and depinning. TCAD simulations demonstrated that the disturb current was reduced by several orders of magnitude with the adaptive barrier gate at Vd = 1 V. These results provide a route toward low-voltage, disturb-immune, and array-scalable capacitorless DRAM.

title: Categorization of Osteoarthritis Using Vision Transformer for Knee X-Ray Analysis
authors: JayapradhaJ, S. L. Jayalakshmi
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Osteoarthritis(OA)is one of the most common cause of mobility limitations in the world and radiographic evaluation is still the main method used in the clinical evaluation of the disease. In practice, knee radiographs are graded according to the Kellgren Lawrence (KL) scale; however, the grading is subjective, time consuming and difficult to standardize in a clinical setting. In order to close this gap, we designed an automated OA severity classification framework that is based on the Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture. The training data used to develop this model and the testing data used to validate the model consisted of 1,525 knee radiographs from open-access sources, including the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI), each of which was classified into KL Grades 0-4. In our experiments, the ViT model was found to better the performance of a ResNet-50 baseline model consistently, with an overall accuracy of 95.2%, and a macro-averaged F1-score of 0.886. The reason for this improvement is because the ViT has a patch-based representation and has the ability to capture long-range patterns in the structure with in the joint space more effectively than CNN. These findings suggest that transformer-based architectures can provide a reliable clinical decision support tool providing more objective and reproducible evaluation of OA severity.

title: TIME: A Taylor-Inspired Mixed-Effects Model for IDH Prediction_supp5-3693918.pdf
authors: Pengwei Hu
publish: N/A
abstract: Background: Intradialytic hypotension (IDH) is a critical complication in hemodialysis that increases morbidity and treatment risks, yet existing machine learning methods inadequately address session-level physiological state heterogeneity and fail to balance linear and nonlinear feature interactions, limiting predictive accuracy and interpretability. Methods: We developed TIME (Taylor-Inspired Mixed-Effects Model), which explicitly separates linear interactions, higher-order nonlinear dependencies, and residual terms inspired by Taylor series expansion, while incorporating a physiological state embedding layer to capture session-level physiological state heterogeneity revealed through hierarchical clustering on autoencoder-derived latent representations of hematologic indicators selected using SHAP analysis and clinical knowledge. Using data from 532 hemodialysis patients (18,309 records), we compared TIME against 19 state-of-the-art baseline models, including blood pressure-stratified subcohort and out-of-distribution analyses. Results: Hierarchical clustering identified two distinct hematological states with different IDH risks (57.93% vs. 55.75%, $p=6.60\times 10^{-7}$). TIME achieved 0.6929 accuracy, 0.7435 F1-score, and 0.3663 MCC on the full cohort, the highest AUC across all three subcohorts (0.7010, 0.6795, and 0.7201), and strong out-of-distribution performance (accuracy 0.7053, F1 0.7377, MCC 0.4009). Integrating TIME's architecture into baseline models improved MCC by 2.67 percentage points on average. Conclusions: TIME effectively predicts IDH by modeling session-level physiological state heterogeneity and balancing feature interactions, with robust generalizability across diverse populations. Significance: TIME advances precision medicine in dialysis care by enabling early risk identification and personalized treatment strategies, while revealing broader immune, nutritional, and fluid-related markers of IDH risk.

title: Magnetic Coupling Path Reconfiguration in Multi-Node Domino Wireless Power Transfer Systems for Insulator Monitoring
authors: Wei Wang, Penghui Li, Zheng Lu, Zihao Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: In domino wireless power transfer (WPT) systems, inefficient magnetic energy circulation arises from complex coupling networks and redundant mutual inductance among resonators, which limits power delivery to downstream nodes, particularly in high-voltage insulator strings with multiple sensors. A magnetic coupling path reconfiguration strategy based on dynamic relay switching is proposed in this paper. By controlling the connection and disconnection of key nodes in the linkage, the target load operates under optimal coupling conditions, enabling controllable energy flow. Results show that, disconnecting node 2 can significantly enhance the downstream coupling strength, increasing load input power by 1.5–2 times, as verified in four- and five-coil structures. A feasible solution for long-term stable power supply to multiple sensors on high-voltage insulator strings is provided.

title: Algorithmic Exploration of Matching Network Topologies for Power Amplifiers
authors: Sergio López de Pablo, Joel Mesas, Pedro de Paco, Jordi Verdú
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: In this article, a novel exploration algorithm for the design and optimization of matching networks (MNs) in power amplifiers (PAs) is presented. The methodology is based on the automatic generation and optimization of both input and output MNs (IMNs/OMNs) according to a set of design rules and constraints that ensure technological feasibility. All candidate MN topologies are optimized and ranked using a unified cost function, enabling the automatic selection of the most suitable solution while facilitating a comprehensive tradeoff analysis among key performance metrics such as power-added efficiency (PAE), gain, bandwidth, physical size, and expected yield. As a result, the designer obtains a global view of the solution space and can objectively compare different MN configurations within a consistent optimization framework. To validate the proposed algorithm, a PA covering 1.1–1.9 GHz—fully encompassing the Galileo navigation bands E5, E6, and L1—and targeting high efficiency with harmonic control, was designed, fabricated, and experimentally characterized.

title: Multi-component Echo Signal Estimation in MIMO Systems Based on the GESR-Atan-Log Nonconvex Regularization
authors: Xiaoting Tang, Yuhang Liang, Zhen Liu, Yuhua Cheng, Jingyuan Wang, Hang Geng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Distinguishing and extracting weak, transient signal components in noisy environments remains a key challenge in radar and communication signal processing. This paper addresses this problem by proposing an advanced generalized enhanced sparse regularization based on Arctangent-Logarithm (GESR-Atan-Log) penalty. The penalty is integrated within a coherent processing framework that includes improved complex variational mode decomposition (ICVMD) and adaptive dictionary learning via K-SVD. Theoretical analysis guarantees the convexity of the cost function under certain conditions, ensuring the global minimum and its stability. Besides, a multi-objective optimization scheme is used to determine hyperparameters, and an efficient approximate solution is provided. Evaluations through simulations and practical RF echo experiments show that GESR-Atan-Log significantly outperforms L1-norm and GMC methods. Quantitative gains include accuracy improvements of 5.389% and 9.998% for the simple point target, and 4.898% and 7.381% for the complex target. In robustness tests across 200 signals with varying noise levels, our method achieved the lowest and most consistent RMSE. These findings conclusively demonstrate the method’s effectiveness for blind source separation, high-fidelity reconstruction, and robust noise suppression.

title: Blockchain-driven Freshness-keeping Efforts in Community Group-buying Platforms_supp1-3694210.docx
authors: Yuqian Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: The loss of freshness-keeping efforts in fresh product supply chains not only diminishes consumers' purchasing experiences but also leads to significant economic losses. This challenge is particularly pronounced in community group-buying (CGB) supply chains. This study investigates how blockchain adoption can influence CGB supply chains, which involve a platform that sources products from the supplier and delegates order fulfillment to the group leader. By leveraging blockchain-enabled real-time traceability, a platform can precisely attribute freshness-keeping efforts to the respective parties, thereby facilitating an incentive mechanism tailored to the effort level. The analysis yields three key findings. First, counterintuitively, blockchain adoption under platform-determined pricing induces a free-riding effect: freshness-keeping efforts are intensified by the group leader and reduced by the supplier. However, the supplier's efforts rebound when pricing power is granted with a higher incentive coefficient. The acquisition of pricing power facilitates blockchain adoption in enhancing supplier's freshness-keeping efforts, yet proves more challenging in improving group leader's freshness-keeping efforts. Second, the platform adopts blockchain only when the traceability cost is sufficiently low and the incentive coefficient remains moderate. Third, lower traceability costs result in a win–win–win (platform-supplier-group leader) outcome, expanding with consumer sensitivity to freshness-keeping efforts. The robustness of these findings is verified through extensive numerical simulations and sensitivity analyses. Furthermore, the optimal incentive coefficient decreases monotonically with the commission rate and exhibits an inverted U-shaped relationship with consumer freshness-keeping effort sensitivity. Our study contributes to existing theoretical frameworks regarding the value of blockchain in fresh product supply chains and provides novel managerial insights for CGB platforms.

title: Energy-efficient train control for metro with onboard energy storage-based external power regulation
authors: Yu Rao, Qiangqiang Liu, Qingyuan Wang, Yujie Lu, Jianping Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: For a DC traction power supply system (TPSS) comprising multiple trains, the coordinated optimization of onboard energy storage (OBES) operations and train control for reducing substation energy consumption poses a significant challenge due to the inherent complexity of the problem’s nonlinear characteristics. In this paper, we adopt the indirect solution methodology outlined in [1]. The power of other trains in the TPSS is represented as the external power for the selected train via power flow calculation. The OBES is employed to achieve active regulation of the external power, and consequently, the optimal control problem, i.e., the energy-efficient train control (EETC) in spatial-temporal area (STA) [1] with OBES-based external power regulation is proposed. The Pontryagin’s Maximum Principle (PMP) is used to analyze the optimal control conditions, and a bi-level iterative solution framework is designed to solve the optimal control problem. Case studies are conducted to show that the proposed method achieves an energy saving of more than 20%.
