title: Instruction-Guided Distribution Maximization for General Few-Shot Intent Recognition_supp1-3692528.pdf
authors: YaJun Du
publish: N/A
abstract: Intent recognition captures the deep needs of users from natural language, and few-shot intent recognition addresses data scarcity. Existing methods treat single- and multi-label intent recognition as separate classification tasks, and discriminative models typically require fine-tuning when generalizing to new intents, which limits generality. To this end, in this study, we re-think the single- and multi-label intent recognition task as a unified natural language generation task rather than a discriminative classification problem. After instruction pre-training on a general intent dataset, the language model (LM) directly generalizes to few-shot target data without fine-tuning. However, limited data and large distribution shifts severely challenge generalization. Therefore, in this paper, we propose an Instruction-Guided Distribution Maximization (IGDM) method to improve the robustness of the model and enhance generalization. IGDM prompts the model to learn a broader decision boundary by enlarging the maximum error of LM between the user utterance and the corresponding intent. Formally, we perform a two-level optimization strategy to obtain the inner instruction-guided sample distribution maximization and the outer model error minimization, respectively. The inner optimization objective is the sample distribution maximization, which is achieved by performing gradient ascent according to LM in instruction learning. The outer optimization objective is the LM, whose optimization is implemented by gradient descent in instruction learning combined with samples from the distribution maximized according to the LM. Extensive theoretical and inference proofs demonstrate the superior robustness and generalization capabilities of IGDM. To verify this, we conduct comprehensive experiments across 20 single-label and 7 multi-label widely used intent recognition benchmarks. The results demonstrate that IGDM achieves superior performance over existing methods across all benchmarks.

title: PEAK: PUF-Based End-to-End Authentication and Key-Exchange for IoT
authors: Chandranshu Gupta, Gaurav Varshney, Gaurav Kumar, Satyadev Ahlawat
publish: IEEE Embedded Systems Letters
abstract: We propose a lightweight end-to-end authentication and key-exchange protocol for resource-constrained IoT devices that avoids computationally expensive cryptographic operations by leveraging Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs) and employs a lightweight Fuzzy Extractor (FE) to reliably handle PUF noise. The protocol relies exclusively on lightweight hash, XOR, and symmetric encryption/decryption operations. Moreover, the proposed protocol mitigates the storage overhead by avoiding the maintenance of large CRP repositories at the server. Security properties are formally verified using the scyther tool. A prototype implementation using two ESP32-based IoT nodes and a server further demonstrates its practicality for real-world deployments. Finally, benchmarking against state-of-the-art schemes indicates that the proposed protocol incurs comparatively low computation time and communication cost, highlighting its efficiency and lightweight nature.

title: Regulation of Multiharmonic Components for Nine-Phase Open-End Winding Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines
authors: Haifeng Wang, Zhiyuan Zhang, Xibo Yuan, Xinzhen Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The nine-phase open-end winding (OW) permanent magnet synchronous machine (PMSM) integrates the advantages of multiphase and OW topologies, making it an alternative solution for high-power propulsions. However, there is still a lack of efficient modulation methods for multiple (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{\geq}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>3) decoupling subspaces to address the multiharmonic current issue in this system. In this article, the advantages of model predictive control in handling multiobjectives and the virtual voltage vector (VVV) technique are utilized to achieve the active regulation of the fundamental, third-, fifth-, and seventh-order components. Unlike existing harmonic suppression strategies in multiphase AC machine drives, this study designs four sets of decoupled VVV to achieve the independent predictive control in four subspaces. Moreover, a multisubspace weight optimization algorithm is proposed to address the loss of harmonic control capability under voltage constraints. The proposed strategy is simple and easy to implement on industrial control platforms. Finally, experimental results validated the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, including a cost-performance comparison of controlling various harmonic components and selecting different constraint modes.

title: A 6G Driven Multiclass Power Efficient Dynamic Bandwidth Allocation (MPE-DBA) Scheme for Passive Optical Network (PON)
authors: Elie Inaty, Charbel Maroun, Ghattas Akkad, Ali Mansour, Martin Maier
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: The latest ITU IMT-2030 recommendations for sixth generation (6G) networks have imposed strict specifications on communication systems. Some of these requirements include increased throughput, ultra-low delay and jitter, differentiated services, and energy efficiency. Current dynamic bandwidth allocation (DBA) schemes for passive optical networks (PON) may meet some of these requirements, yet they fail to fulfill other recommendations, especially energy efficiency. Therefore, we propose a new PON architecture, whose objective is to offer flexibility in meeting the IMT-2030 recommendations. It uses a multiclass power efficient dynamic bandwidth allocation (MPE-DBA) scheme that helps achieving both differentiated services and sustainability in terms of energy and cost. For computational efficiency, we propose a two stages Mamdani fuzzy inference system (FIS). The inputs of the first FIS are the latency and cost of the 6G traffic, whereas the latency and cost of the non-6G (N6G) traffic are the inputs of the second FIS stage. Both FISs use the variation in the number of channels as output. The proposed algorithm achieves less than 100 μs delay, less than 10μs jitter and high aggregate throughput for the 6G packets. In addition, it reduces the power consumption by three times and the cost of traffic transmission by four times as compared to the state-of-the art solution.

title: IEEE Brain Hosts 2025 IEEE Brain Discovery and Neurotechnology Workshop [Society News]
authors: Cynthia Weber, Vesna Sossi, Ljiljana Trajkovic
publish: IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine
abstract: Advances in understanding of the brain in health and disease is leading to groundbreaking discoveries and engineering solutions. Development and deployment of effective neurotechnology and means of studying the brain through neuroimaging techniques and machine learning requires an integrated approach as well as close collaboration among the neuroengineering community, neuroscientists, and clinical practitioners. The 2025 IEEE Brain Discovery and Neurotechnology Workshop brought together researchers and practitioners across academia, industry, and the clinical profession, highlighted innovative neurotechnology and brain research methods and fostered opportunities for cross-disciplinary collaboration.

title: Machine Learning Enabled Movement Pattern Recognition and Monitoring Using a Pomegranate Membrane Based Sensor_supp1-3692686.docx
authors: Harminder Singh
publish: N/A
abstract: Wearable Intelligent activity recognition has emerged as a rapidly growing research area with significant relevance to ubiquitous computing and intelligent human–computer interaction, enabling applications in healthcare, fitness assessment, gaming, tactical military systems, and indoor navigation. Wearable sensing technologies, particularly triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG)–based sensors, have gained considerable attention due to their self-powered operation, mechanical flexibility, stability, and cost-effectiveness. In this work, a biodegradable and sustainable triboelectric nanogenerator sensor is designed and developed to promote environmentally friendly electronic systems. The proposed device utilizes a pomegranate membrane as the primary triboelectric material, resulting in a biodegradable pomegranate membrane–based TENG (PM-TENG). The PM-TENG demonstrates a maximum output voltage of 48.3 V and is capable of charging multiple commercial capacitors. Furthermore, the sensor effectively captures, monitors, and distinguishes human activities, including walking, running, and jumping. To enhance activity recognition performance, a machine learning–based framework is introduced for efficient signal processing and accurate classification of human activities. The integration of machine learning models with the PM-TENG sensor shows superior performance, with the histogram gradient boosting (HGB) model achieving the highest classification accuracy of 97.78%. These results highlight the potential of the proposed PM-TENG sensor as a sustainable, self-powered, and intelligent wearable platform for high-precision human activity monitoring.

title: Ultra-Wideband, Polarization-Insensitive, and Angular-Stable Microwave Absorber Based on ITO-Deposited FSS
authors: Awanish Kumar
publish: IEEE Letters on Electromagnetic Compatibility Practice and Applications
abstract: This work presents a polarization insensitive, highly angular stable, and ultrawideband microwave absorber operating over 2.1-25 GHz. The proposed absorber uses a periodic array of vertically arranged lossy frequency selective surface (FSS) patterns. The presence of losses inside the resonators with multiple resonant modes enables a wide absorption band. Moreover, the symmetry within the FSS unit cells ensures the polarization insensitivity. The subwavelength periodicity of the unit cell, along with vertically stacked FSS layers, contributes to high angular stability. Based on simulation analysis, the proposed absorber achieves more than 90% absorption over 2.1–25 GHz for incidence angles up to 60°, while maintaining an ultra-thin thickness of 0.078 λL and a fractional bandwidth of 169%, offering an improved trade-off among bandwidth, thickness, and angular stability compared to existing FSS-based absorbers. Furthermore, the absorber is fabricated with three Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)-engraved FSS layers, foam, and FR-4 substrates and characterized in an anechoic chamber. The experimental results verify the absorber’s high performance. Theoretical and experimental investigations confirm that the proposed design holds strong potential for radar cross-section (RCS) mitigation and electromagnetic interference suppression.

title: A Secure Machine Learning-Based Framework for Aggressive Driving Behavior Analysis in IoV System
authors: SAKSHAM MITTAL, MOHAMMAD WAZID, ASHOK KUMAR DAS, DEVESH PRATAP SINGH, VIVEKANANDA BHAT K, Sunil Prajapat
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: The increase in the number of road accidents is one of the major global concerns nowadays. The aggressive and abnormal driving behavior of the drivers is the primary cause of this issue. Such behavior can be observed frequently in urban traffic environments, leading to severe consequences including injuries, fatalities, and property damage. Therefore, it is very important to have a certain intelligent mechanism that can monitor driving behavior in real time and take appropriate action if it is found to be aggressive or abnormal. This can be possible by integrating the Internet of Things (IoT), digital twins, and machine learning technology. In this paper, we have proposed a secure machine learning-based framework for the detection of aggressive driving behavior in Internet of Vehicles (IoV) systems. It is assumed that smart IoT computing devices, such as sensors and cameras, are installed on roads and in moving vehicles to capture data such as sudden acceleration, harsh braking, sharp turns, tailgating, etc. This data is sent to the cloud servers in a secure manner, incorporating mutual authentication and key establishment between communicating entities. Machine learning models that are deployed on cloud servers are trained and tested against such data to analyze driving patterns. The machine learning classifiers that are utilized in the proposed work are Random Forest, K-nearest neighbor, AdaBoost, and Multi-layer perceptron (MLP) classifier, where Random Forest has achieved the maximum accuracy and F1-score. Finally, the proposed scheme is also compared with other existing schemes based on different parameters and outperforms those schemes.

title: Principled Uncertainty Decomposition with Bayesian Ensemble Transformers for Trustworthy Intrusion Detection_supp1-3694131.pdf
authors: Xiufeng Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Network intrusion detection systems (IDS) are critical for cybersecurity, yet existing approaches often provide overconfident predictions without reliable uncertainty estimates, hindering critical decision-making. We introduce a novel Bayesian ensemble transformer framework for uncertainty-aware intrusion detection that delivers both strong detection performance and well-calibrated confidence. Our framework uniquely combines transformer architectures with ensemble methods to achieve a principled decomposition of prediction uncertainty into epistemic (model uncertainty) and aleatoric (data uncertainty) components, offering actionable insights for security analysts. We establish theoretical convergence guarantees for this approach under local convexity assumptions, supported by empirical validation. Comprehensive experiments across four benchmark datasets (NSL-KDD, CICIDS2017, UNSW-NB15, and SWaT) demonstrate F1-scores ranging from 77.55% to 97.00%, with particularly strong performance on UNSW-NB15 (97.00%), alongside excellent calibration with Expected Calibration Error (ECE) ranging from 0.0248 to 0.2278. Adversarial robustness analysis shows minimal performance degradation under sophisticated C&W (3.6% drop) and PGD attacks (8.0% drop), highlighting the framework's resilience. This work advances intrusion detection by providing interpretable uncertainty and robust performance, addressing a critical gap in current cybersecurity systems to enable more informed and reliable security decisions.

title: Safe Optimal Formation of Continuous-Time Multiagent Systems via Maximum Entropy Adaptive Dynamic Programming
authors: Lulu Zhang, Huaguang Zhang, Tianbiao Wang, Jiawei Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: This article addresses the problem of formation control for multiagent systems operating in partially unknown dynamic environments. A safe optimal control method is proposed by employing maximum entropy adaptive dynamic programming (MEADP) in continuous-time systems, alongside high-order control barrier functions (HoCBFs). First, an MEADP algorithm for continuous-time systems is developed to encourage exploration and prevent convergence to suboptimal solutions under partially unknown system dynamics, with a comprehensive convergence proof of the algorithm provided. Then, safety constraints are formulated using HoCBFs, which are capable of handling safety constraints with higher relative degrees. These constraints are incorporated via quadratic programming to modify the maximum entropy optimal control policy, ensuring that the system states remain within a predefined safe set and effectively avoid collisions with obstacles. Moreover, the MEADP method utilizes a critic neural network to approximate the entropy-regularized Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman equation, leading to a maximum entropy optimal control policy. Finally, simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach, making it suitable for safe formation control tasks in complex dynamic environments.

title: Multimodal Patient-Specific Identification of Atrial Flutter Circuits from ECG Time Series Using Explainable Machine Learning
authors: Samuel Ruipérez-Campillo, David Hernando, Elisa Ramírez, Sergio Castrejón, Cecilia Zapata, Carlos Rodríguez Carneiro, Julia E. Vogt, José Luis Merino, Francisco Castells, José Millet
publish: IEEE Journal of Translational Engineering in Health and Medicine
abstract: Objective: Accurate pre-procedural identification of atrial flutter (AFL) mechanisms can streamline mapping and indirectly inform ablation strategy, yet surface-electrocardiogram (ECG) criteria remain unreliable and circuit definition is typically confirmed invasively. Methods and procedures: We analyzed 97 consecutive patients undergoing electrophysiological (EP) study with simultaneous 12-lead ECG and EP-verified AFL subtype; adenosine-induced atrioventricular AV block enabled extraction of clean atrial segments. We reconstructed atrial vectorcardiograms (VCGs) and engineered interpretable descriptors of loop morphology and kinematics, including archetype cosine correlation, geometric complexity, and velocity-based slow-occupancy indices, then fused these with clinical variables in an explainable tree-ensemble model evaluated with nested cross-validation. Results: VCG loops exhibited subtype-specific archetypes (within-class correlation: 0.832±0.129 C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CCW</sub>, 0.874±0.154 C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub>, 0.647±0.127 PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CCW</sub>, 0.667±0.159 PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub>; C: common; PM: perimitral; CW: clockwise; CCW: counter-CW). On the test set, the multimodal Random-Forest improved discrimination over VCG-only and clinical-only baselines, achieving AUROC of 0.870 (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CCW</sub>), 0.900 (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub>), 0.840 (PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CCW</sub>), and 0.790 (PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub>), with high sensitivity for common AFL (0.833 and 0.929) and very high specificity for PM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CW</sub> (0.988). Conclusion: This interpretable framework provides a practical route to non-invasive, mechanism-oriented AFL stratification to support targeted mapping and more efficient ablation planning. Future work will focus on multicentre prospective validation and robust atrial-signal extraction without adenosine to broaden routine applicability. Clinical and Translational Impact Statement: Clinical Research. An interpretable ECG+clinical model predicts AFL mechanism pre-procedure, guiding mapping and ablation strategy. Integration: export 12-lead ECG, compute VCG features, and display subtype probability with key drivers in the EP lab.

title: Video-Centered Multimodal Learning for Non-Invasive Detection of Emotional Workload in Emotional Labor Settings
authors: Yejin Choi, Yejin Jang, Eunji Park
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Workers in emotional labor environments often followdisplay rules that require them to express emotions as directed by their boss or the company, regardless of their true feelings. These workers suppress their true feelings, which leads to ongoing mental issues and an accumulation of emotional workload. For example, in a call center situation, if a customer is shouting and making accusatory comments at the worker, the worker should respond in a positive way to the customer, even if they have negative feelings. However, it still remains difficult issue to estimate emotional workload in real-world settings using non-invasive methods. In this study, we propose a machine learning model that automatically detects workers’ emotional workload in emotional labor situations using multimodal data, including video and physiological data. We used a dataset designed based on a call center scenario and estimated emotional workload using two label: an objective label based on experimental conditions, and a subjective label based on workers’ self-reports. As a result, we found that geometric features derived from facial expressions captured in videos significantly contributed to model performance and that applying MultipleKernel Learning (MKL) improved classification accuracy, especially when detecting subjective emotional workload. This study demonstrates the potential of image-based and MKL-based models for scalable and non-invasive detection of emotional workload, suggesting that they can be effectively applied in real-world work settings.

title: AURA: A Diagnostic Framework for Tracking User Satisfaction of Interactive Planning Agents
authors: Takyoung Kim, Janvijay Singh, Shuhaib Mehri, Emre Can Acikgoz, Sagnik Mukherjee, Nimet Beyza Bozdag, Sumuk Shashidhar, Gokhan Tur, Dilek Hakkani-Tür
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: The growing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in instruction-following and context-understanding have led to the emergence of agentic systems with numerous applications. Among these, task planning agents have become especially prominent in realistic scenarios involving complex internal pipelines, such as context understanding, tool management, and response generation. However, existing benchmarks predominantly evaluate agent performance based on task completion as a proxy for overall effectiveness. We hypothesize that merely improving task completion is misaligned with maximizing user satisfaction, as users interact with the entire agentic process and not just the end result. To address this gap, we propose <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">AURA</small>, an <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">A</u>gent-<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">U</u>ser inte<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</u>action <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">A</u>ssessment framework that conceptualizes the behavioral stages of interactive task planning agents. <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">AURA</small> offers a comprehensive assessment of agents through a set of atomic LLM evaluation criteria, allowing researchers and practitioners to diagnose specific strengths and weaknesses within the agent's decision-making pipeline. Our analyses show that agents excel at different behavioral stages, with user satisfaction shaped by both outcomes and intermediate behaviors. We also highlight future directions, including systems that leverage multiple agents and the limitations of user simulators in task planning.

title: Comprehensive Strain Study of Monolithic Nanosheet CFETs
authors: Ying-Qi Liu, Hung-Chun Chou, Tao Chou, Xuan-Hao Wu, Bo-Wei Huang, Chien-Te Tu, Yi-Chun Liu, C. W. Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: The step-by-step evolution of strain<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\textit {xx}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> along the current direction of monolithic nanosheet (NS) complementary FET (CFET) is studied through finite element analysis (FEA). The defective merge of source/drain regrowth is modeled by an air gap, while the defect-free merge of S/D regrowth is modeled by seamless S/D. After channel release, the bottom Si<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.8</sub> Ge<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.2</sub> pFET with seamless Si<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.6</sub> Ge<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.4</sub> S/D is compressively strained, while the top Si nFET with seamed Si:P (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3\times 10^{{21}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{-{3}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) S/D is also slightly compressively strained due to inward bending of Si:P S/D regrowth. Seamless Si:P/Si<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.95</sub> C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.05</sub> and seamed Si<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.8</sub> Ge<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.2</sub> S/D can provide the desired tensile strain<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\textit {xx}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> to top nFETs to boost mobility. The seamless Si:P and seamless Si<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.6</sub> Ge<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.4</sub> S/D of nFET and pFET, respectively, can provide strain-enhanced mobility and uniform inter-NS strain regardless of the n on p or p on n placement.

title: Precision Docking Adaptive Clamp Claw Design Based on Edge Calculation
authors: Boming Li, Wangzhun Chu, Helong Li, Sheng Hua, Guangze Zhu, Gong Chen, Jie Huang, Shunming Teng
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Smart Energy and Control Engineering (SECE)
abstract: Due to the advantages of small size, less footprint and high reliability, GIS equipment has gradually replaced the original open switching equipment (AIS) equipment, which is widely used in substations and converter stations of various voltage levels, and has become the most important form of electrical equipment. For the installation of GIS equipment, there is an urgent need for a mechanized equipment to achieve high-precision docking, replacing the backward is the construction technology. This paper proposes an adaptive clamclaw structure based on edge calculation, which can overcome the problem of large weight and high precision clamping and install it on the multi-degree of freedom robotic arm to complete GIS installation.

title: SoilWalk: An IoT-Enabled Smart Slipper System for Real-Time Soil Health Mapping
authors: Mary Shamala L, Tracita H
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Soil health plays a big role in deciding how well crops grow and how much they yield. Many farmers still face issues because they do not have easy or affordable ways to check their soil condition regularly. SoilWalk is a smart slipper system designed to make this process simple and handy. The slipper has built-in sensors that can measure soil moisture, temperature, and TDS levels while walking on the field. The data is sent wirelessly to a cloud platform using IoT and is shown on a dashboard in real time. Based on the readings, the system helps to identify if the soil is in good, moderate, or poor condition. This idea mainly focuses on helping farmers understand their soil better without depending on lab tests or expensive equipment. It is low-cost, portable, and easy to use, making it a good fit for small and local farmers to improve their crop management and yield.

title: RecognAVSE-V2: An Improved Cross-Attention-Based Audio-Visual Speech Enhancement Approach
authors: Leandro A. Passos, João R. Manesco, Rahma Fourati, João P. Papa, Amir Hussain
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Complex Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICCSIC)
abstract: Audio-visual speech enhancement (AVSE) is a sub-field of machine learning that aims to improve speech quality from noisy audio signals, leveraging visual cues to guide information flow and enhance learning. In this context, the COG-MHEAR program supports an early competition, namely the AVSE Challenge (AVSEC), to advance the development of new solutions and AVSE methods while fostering the community. This paper introduces the RecognAVSE-V2, an improved and more efficient version of RecognAVSE proposed at AVSEC 2024, that implements a Time-Synced Cross-Attention Mechanism that models the temporal dependencies between both modalities. Its architecture consists of a video encoder for spatiotemporal feature learning, an audio encoder operating on STFT representations, and a time-synchronized cross-attention module that aligns audio and video features. Experimental results conducted over AVSE Challenge and CHiME3 datasets show RecognAVSE-V2 is capable of outperforming the baseline and its prior version in some cases while consisting of a model with reduced complexity, 20 times smaller, whose inference is 26% faster, and training demands a quarter of the total time.

title: EFL Learners’ Acceptance of a Game-Based Digital Platform for Grammar Learning: A Technology Acceptance Study
authors: Nuong To Huynh
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: The aim of the study was to investigate EFL (English as a Foreign Language) students’ acceptance of using Wordwall platform in learning Grammar at a private university in The Mekong Delta of Vietnam. This study examines students’ acceptance in terms of perceived ease of use, perceived usefulness, and behavioral intention to use. The mixed-method design was used in this study. The participants of the study were 103 EFL students attending in English preparation courses which were compulsory in their university curriculum. The SPSS 25 program was used to analyze the data. The study employed a five-point Likert scale questionnaire and a semi-structured interview for data collection. The findings showed that students’ perceptions on the use of Wordwall platform in their grammar classes were positive. This study also explores what motivates student’s acceptance of using this platform to learn grammar. Implications for practical platforms of Wordwall were addressed in this study to enhance EFL students’ grammatical knowledge.

title: Analyzing Usage Patterns of Artificial Intelligence Tools Among Criminology Students Using Machine Learning and Behavioral Analytics
authors: Hyrizel Banhi Umhao, Jordan L. Mariano, Isaac Angelo Dioses
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: This study analyzes the usage patterns of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools among criminology students through the integration of behavioral analytics, sentiment analysis, and machine learning techniques. A dataset consisting of 533 respondents from Region II and Region III was collected using a structured survey instrument with Likert-scale and open-ended responses. Feature engineering was applied to construct composite behavioral indices, while sentiment analysis was performed to extract attitudinal insights from textual data. The processed dataset was used to train and evaluate classification models, including Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN). The results show that the SVM model achieved the highest performance, with an accuracy exceeding 95%, precision reaching 100%, and recall values up to 100% for the Moderate class. The LDA model also demonstrated strong performance with AUC values ranging from 0.983 to 0.997, while KNN showed comparatively lower performance with recall values as low as 69% for the High class. Feature importance analysis using the mRMR algorithm identified the AI Usage Index (0.3682) as the most significant predictor, followed by usage frequency and tools used. These findings highlight the effectiveness of integrating machine learning and sentiment analysis in modeling AI usage behavior and supporting data-driven educational strategies

title: A Hybrid Metaheuristic Approach to the VRPTW in Home Healthcare Logistics
authors: Ahlem Fkiri, Neila Rjaibi, Mohamed Salim Amri Sakhri
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Complex Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICCSIC)
abstract: The Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (VRPTW) is a well-known challenge in transport logistics, particularly critical in home healthcare services where adhering to patient-specific time constraints is essential. This paper presents a practical and computationally efficient hybrid approach that integrates classical optimization techniques with lightweight artificial intelligence methods. Our methodology begins with K-means clustering to group patients based on their time availability, creating logical clusters for scheduling. Within each cluster, initial routes are generated using the NN algorithm, which are then refined through TS(Tabu search) to further minimize travel distance and improve adherence to time windows. This three-phase approach balances solution quality with computational simplicity, making it especially suitable for scenarios where operational resources and processing time are constrained. Experimental results on simulated datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive route optimization while maintaining feasibility with respect to patient time windows. These results suggest strong potential for practical implementation in real-world home healthcare logistics and similar time-sensitive delivery environments.

title: Design of Robotic Arm Control System Based on Fuzzy PID Algorithm
authors: Tianyu Wu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Robot Perception and Intelligent Control (RPIC)
abstract: Addressing the nonlinear and strongly coupled characteristics of robotic manipulators, along with the poor robustness and limited adaptability of traditional PID control under complex operating conditions, this paper presents the designed of a robotic arm control system based on a fuzzy PID algorithm. Initially, the hardware of the robotic arm was divided into four core modules, clarifying the structure and collaborative logic of each component. Subsequently, a fuzzy PID controller is constructed, employing a six-step core process to achieve dynamic tuning of PID parameters. Finally, a simulation model of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) with load disturbance rejection is built using Matlab/Simulink to compare the control performance of traditional PID and fuzzy PID. The simulation results demonstrate that the fuzzy PID algorithm can reduce the three-phase current imbalance of the motor from 8.3% to 3.1%, significantly suppress torque and speed overshoot, substantially shorten disturbance recovery time, and limit steadystate fluctuations of the system to only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 0 \%} \boldsymbol{\sim} \mathbf{2 5 \%}$</tex> of those observed with traditional PID. This algorithm effectively enhances the dynamic response speed and disturbance rejection capability of the robotic arm drive system, overcoming the limitations of traditional PID in nonlinear systems and providing a viable solution for optimizing control algorithms for robotic arms in high-end intelligent equipment.

title: A Cloud-Edge Collaborative Task Offloading Strategy for Cost Optimization in Remote Field Scenarios
authors: Jinhuai Yang, Zhiyuan He, Jing Chen, Xiaojuan Yang, Jianchen Lin, Fenzeng Chen, Qianling Zheng, Qifan Yang, Qianwen Wu, Yuzhong Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: Cloud-edge collaborative computing is increasingly applied in complex scenarios like field observation. However, large-scale heterogeneous tasks from terminal devices pose significant challenges for resource scheduling. To address this, a composite wireless channel model is constructed to accurately characterize field environments, incorporating path loss, shadowing, and Rician fading. Based on this, a system cost optimization model is established, considering task latency, storage, and bandwidth constraints. Subsequently, we propose an Adaptive Cooperative Genetic Algorithm-Particle Swarm Optimization (AC-GAPSO) to minimize the overall system cost. AC-GAPSO synergizes the crossover and mutation mechanisms of Genetic Algorithms (GA) with the rapid convergence characteristics of Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). Specifically, a topology-aware scheduling strategy is employed to strictly satisfy task dependency, while an adaptive cooperative mechanism is introduced to enhance global search performance, incorporating a penalty function-based fitness evaluation and feasibility-priority ranking to ensure solution feasibility. Experimental results show that AC-GAPSO outperforms PSO, GA, and Greedy algorithms, achieving an average total cost reduction of 42.02%, validating its effectiveness in field collaborative computing.

title: Memory Non-Sharing and Context Fragmentation in Multi-Agent Systems: Problems and Solutions
authors: Zhengwen He, Mingyong Yin, Dewen Kong, Yubing Duan, Guoxin Zhong, Yulong Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems show great potential in collaborative complex tasks. However, limited by fixed context windows and flawed memory mechanisms, they often suffer from issues such as memory non-sharing, context fragmentation, and hallucination cascades, which severely undermine collaboration effectiveness. Existing studies have alleviated some problems through shared memory, knowledge graphs, and reflection mechanisms. Nevertheless, reflection mostly focuses on correcting current errors, lacking explicit modeling and long-term memory of error causes and failure patterns, leading systems to potentially repeat mistakes in similar scenarios. Building on the framework of shared structured graph memory and reflective update, this paper proposes a multi-agent reflection mechanism incorporating failure pattern memory. The core idea is: during the reflection phase, not only identify and correct erroneous facts and inconsistent reasoning paths, but also extract error types, root causes, and typical context patterns. These are encoded as nodes (e.g., ErrorEvent, RootCause, MitigationRule, ContextPattern) and relational edges in the graph, forming a retrievable failure pattern subgraph. In subsequent task planning, tool calling, and multi-agent collaboration, the system can proactively retrieve these failure pattern memories and inject corresponding preventive rules and risk prompts into the current context, thereby achieving a shift from "post-hoc error correction" to "pre-emptive prevention". We present the complete system architecture and collaboration process, discuss potential advantages compared to traditional multi-agent memory and reflection mechanisms, and design corresponding baseline comparison schemes and evaluation metrics. This framework is expected to yield significant gains in reducing hallucination repetition rates, minimizing cross-agent conflicts, improving task success rates, and enhancing long-term adaptive capabilities.

title: SPPFuzz: A State-Aware Process Pool Based Black-Box Fuzzing Framework for Network Protocols
authors: Yining Liuli, Yan Cao, Xuan Qi
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: The throughput of stateful network protocol fuzzing is fundamentally constrained by initialization overhead, full-state resets, and kernel protocol stack latency. This paper presents SPPFuzz, a black-box framework that addresses these limitations through three core mechanisms. First, the State-Aware Process Pool employs Dirty-Page-Aware Snapshot Recovery (DPASR) to minimize reset costs by tracking soft-dirty pages. Second, Protocol Semantic Mapping reconstructs network interface semantics in user space to achieve zero-copy communication. Third, Memory Behavior Feedback (MBF) infers internal protocol states by exploiting dirty-page distribution characteristics. Experiments on ProFuzzBench demonstrate that SPPFuzz achieves 17.37× to 38.20× throughput improvement over AFLNet. Furthermore, the framework outperforms the baseline by triggering 4.3× more unique crashes in total and discovering over 3× more unique states.

title: A Wideband Q/V Band Cascode Darlington Power Amplifier Using Transmission Line Transformer and Current-Reuse Techniques
authors: Chin-Wei Kuo, Hsin-Chieh Lin, Chien-Nan Kuo, Hwann-Kaeo Chiou
publish: 2026 IEEE 29th International Symposium on Design and Diagnostics of Electronic Circuits and Systems (DDECS)
abstract: This paper presents an enhanced current-reuse cascode Darlington broadband power amplifier using the cost effective tsmc <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{\text {TM }} 90-\text{nm}$</tex> CMOS process. It employs a modified Darlington architecture and a transmission-line transformer for ultra-broadband performance. Measurements show a 16.4 dB small-signal gain with a 3-dB bandwidth of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$27 \text{GHz}(33-60 \text{GHz})$</tex>, achieving a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 8 \%}$</tex> fractional bandwidth. The amplifier delivers a wideband flat saturated output power (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{P}_{\text {sat }}$</tex>) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9.9 \pm 0.6 \text{dBm}$</tex> from 35 to 60 GHz. The total chip area is only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.82 ~\text{mm}^{2}$</tex>.

title: 3D UAV Path Planning and Sensitivity Analysis Based on Artificial Life Algorithm
authors: Luda Zhen
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Smart Energy and Control Engineering (SECE)
abstract: D path planning is the core technical link to realize unmanned and autonomous operation, and the traditional unmanned and path planning algorithms are easy to fall into the problem of local optimization and slow convergence speed. A simulation of motion, reproduction, mutation and natural selection artificial life algorithm is proposed for 3D path planning of UAV in complex static and dynamic environment. By constructing a three-dimensional simulation environment that integrates static and dynamic obstacles, the fitness function of fusing path length and collision penalty is designed, and the sensitivity analysis of the core parameters (variation, survival, and reproduction) of the algorithm is carried out by combining cubic spline interpolation and bilinear difference, and the influence of parameters on the path planning quality and convergence speed is studied, and the parameter combination is determined to effectively balance the global path efficiency and local obstacle avoidance ability. This study provides reliable algorithm scheme and data support for real-time autonomous path planning of UAVs in complex dynamic scenarios, and also lays the foundation for multi-UAV collaborative planning.

title: SmartNav: Natural Language-Based Interaction with MapRoutes
authors: Prashanna Kumar S, Rajesh G, Navaneetha Krishnan U, Vijaya Lakshmi A
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Traditional navigation systems are very good at what they were originally designed to do: compute routes that minimize time or distance. The problem is that this narrow focus does not always translate into a satisfying experience for real users navigating real cities. Urban road networks are layered and irregular, shaped by flyovers, tunnels, service roads, and temporary constraints that are difficult to flatten into a simple graph. When navigation systems fail to account for these structures, the result is often vague or confusing instructions, which in turn increase cognitive load and user frustration.This paper introduces SmartNav, an intelligent navigation assistant that attempts to close this gap by treating communication and structure as first-class concerns rather than afterthoughts. SmartNav tightly integrates Natural Language Understanding (NLU), structurally aware graph-based routing, and context-sensitive Natural Language Generation (NLG). Users can express nuanced preferences in everyday language, while the routing engine explicitly models multi-level urban infrastructure during both route computation and instruction generation. The aim is not only to reach a destination efficiently, but to explain the route in a way that aligns with how people actually perceive and describe their surroundings.We evaluate SmartNav using real-world road networks from the Chennai Metropolitan Area, a setting that captures many of the complexities typical of large, rapidly evolving cities. The system achieves an intent classification accuracy of 89.2 percent and satisfies user-specified constraints in 94.3 percent of cases. Complex natural language query interpretation achieves a success rate of 82.6 percent. Participants report an average satisfaction score of 4.3 out of 5, suggesting that the generated instructions are both clear and trustworthy. Cognitive burden reduction, measured through a validated NASA-TLX workload scale, showed a 31.4 percent decrease compared to conventional navigation interfaces. The end-to-end system maintains a low average response latency of 1.8 seconds. Taken together, these results suggest that SmartNav delivers navigation that is not only accurate, but also interpretable and better aligned with human expectations in complex urban environments.

title: Vision Transformer Combined with Force Feedback for Flexible Object Grasping and Recognition in Robotic Arms
authors: Junqi Ma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing and Automation (IMA)
abstract: In response to the bottleneck of accurately recognizing dynamic deformation and interaction states when a single visual modality is used to grasp flexible objects by a robotic arm, this paper proposes a Vision Tactile Fusion Network (VTFN) based on force guided visual attention. The research aims to address the problem of insufficient deep dynamic correlation mining between visual global deformation and instantaneous physical signals of force perception using existing multimodal methods. The method adopts a dual stream encoding architecture: the visual stream extracts spatial feature maps and global representations containing global deformation context based on the MobileViT-v2 (Mobile Vision Transformer) backbone network; Force flow uses temporal convolutional networks to encode the physical dynamic characteristics of force/torque sequences. This article proposes a directional cross modal fusion module that uses encoded force sensing features as queries to actively retrieve the most relevant regions to the current physical interaction in visual spatial features. Through attention mechanisms, precise adaptive modal alignment and feature fusion are achieved. On the self built “FlexiGrass Dynamic” dataset, VTFN achieved an accuracy of 92.7% and an average F1 score of 0.915. The ablation experiment confirmed the effectiveness of each module, and the noise robustness and cross object generalization tests further showed that the method maintained high recognition accuracy under noise interference, with an average accuracy of 84.0% on unseen objects, demonstrating superior practicality and generalization ability.

title: Cooperative Optimization Method for Intersection Signals Based on Transformer-BEV Representation and QMIX Value Decomposition Reinforcement Learning
authors: Shun Li
publish: 2026 International Conference on Robot Perception and Intelligent Control (RPIC)
abstract: As urban traffic networks become increasingly complex, achieving intelligent coordinated optimization of intersection signals has become a key issue in intelligent transportation systems. This paper proposes an intersection signal coordination optimization method based on Transformer-BEV representation and QMIX value decomposition reinforcement learning. Building on the existing BEV representation, the method introduces a Transformer encoder-decoder structure to effectively extract global spatiotemporal features among multiple intersections. The extracted features are then input into the QMIX multi-agent reinforcement learning framework, treating each intersection as an agent to achieve distributed signal control. To address the limitations of traditional QMIX in modeling complex inter-agent dependencies, this paper designs an Adaptive Attention QMIX (AAQMIX) module, which adaptively adjusts value decomposition weights through a dynamic traffic interaction graph learning mechanism, thereby enhancing the model's robustness and generalization capability in dynamic traffic scenarios. Experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing mainstream methods in terms of average waiting time, traffic throughput, and inter-intersection coordination.

title: Research on Adaptive Teaching for Students in the Initial Primary School Art Class—AIGC-Assisted Teaching Practice in the "Introducing Myself" Lesson
authors: Meng Jia, He Gao, Xiuli Sun, Jingting Ma
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: Student adaptability plays a crucial role in establishing subject cognition and interest. The first primary school art class serves as a critical starting point for students' artistic learning journey. In response to widespread student adaptation challenges, this study proposes a shift in teaching from passively addressing problems to proactively nurturing potential. An adaptive teaching framework encompassing three dimensions — emotional ice-breaking, behavioral norm-setting, and relational connection-building—is constructed. Subsequently, teaching practices assisted by AIGC in the "Introducing Myself" lesson are implemented, thereby transforming potential "adaptation crisis points" in the first art class into "developmental opportunities".

title: Leveraging Self-Supervised Learning as Features in Audio Deepfake Detection
authors: Jean Alex Firmino Pinhata, Douglas Rodrigues, Alex M. G. de Almeida, Kelton Costa, João Paulo Papa
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Complex Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICCSIC)
abstract: This paper investigates the relative effectiveness of traditional acoustic features and self-supervised learning (SSL) embeddings for audio deepfake detection on the Fake-or-Real (FoR) corpus. We evaluate nine feature sets — MFCC, CQCC, RMS, ZCR, Teager, Wav2Vec2, HuBERT, WavLM, and Whisper, using Accuracy, F1-score, and Equal Error Rate (EER) as metrics. At the family level, Handcrafted and SSL features exhibit very similar distributions; quartiles and Mann–Whitney U tests reveal no statistically significant differences in Accuracy, F1, or EER (p > 0.90), indicating that SSL embeddings do not globally outperform handcrafted representations.At the feature level, however, clear patterns emerge, Whisper and MFCC are the most reliable features, with median accuracies of 0.9602 and 0.9571 and median EER of 0.0375 and 0.0391, respectively, without significant differences between them. WavLM forms a second tier with competitive but weaker results. Overall, the results show that cepstral features such as MFCC and CQCC remain robust options and that SSL embeddings, particularly Whisper and WavLM, complement rather than replace well-designed handcrafted features in audio deepfake detection.

title: Multi-Parameter Fusion Evaluation and Dynamic Binning Scheduling for Energy Storage Systems
authors: Chenrui Tang, Sipeng Hao, Yurui Li, Yue Yuan
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Smart Energy and Control Engineering (SECE)
abstract: The inherent inconsistency among individual battery cells leads to the degradation of the overall performance of battery energy storage systems (BESS). This paper proposes a multi-parameter fusion and dynamic binning scheduling strategy to improve battery consistency and optimize system performance. Firstly, a multi-parameter battery state coefficient is constructed based on the State of Charge (SOC), State of Health (SOH), internal resistance, and temperature, which enhances the accuracy and comprehensiveness of battery performance evaluation. Performance calculation formulas for individual cells in both charging and discharging states are also derived. Secondly, cells are categorized into different bins based on their performance; to account for performance variations during operation, a dynamic adjustment mechanism combining event-triggered and timetriggered controls is implemented. Finally, battery power is allocated across different bins according to power response requirements, with limiting and derating constraints integrated to ensure scheduling stability. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces SOC dispersion and promotes the convergence of battery state coefficients. The strategy maintains the stability of the binning structure and power allocation across multiple scenarios, achieving continuous improvement in battery cluster consistency and providing technical support for the efficient operation of BESS.

title: Deep Learning based Automated Detection of Bacterial Keratitis from Corneal Images
authors: Sathishkumar M, Abitha A, Jeyiin Keerthani C, Abishai M, Arun Kumar T, Kiruthikha M
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Bacterial keratitis is the serious corneal infection that rapidly leads to vision impairment or permanent blindness if the disease is not diagnosed at its early stage. Thus, the accurate detection of disease from the corneal images is challenging due to variations in lesion appearance, illumination conditions and image noise present in clinical images. To address the challenges, this research proposed the automated bacterial keratitis detection model using ConvNeXt–Efficient Transformer Network (CETNet) with the optimization strategy for improved learning performance. Initially, corneal images obtained from the publicly available dataset are preprocessed using Gaussian filter to remove noise and enhance structural features. The enhanced images are then fed into the CETNet architecture, wherein the feed-forward network (FFN) layer is enhanced using the hybrid ConvNeXt-EfficientNet module for obtaining improved spatial and hierarchical feature extraction. Besides, the loss function is optimized using the proposed Chaotic Beaver Behavior Optimization (C2BO) algorithm, which integrates chaotic Chebyshev mapping within the exploration phase of the Beaver Behavior Optimization algorithm to improve convergence and avoid local minima. The experimental analysis of the proposed model demonstrated the improved classification accuracy and robustness for automated bacterial keratitis detection.

title: Preclinical Assessment of an Image-Guided Histotripsy System for Renal Ablation_supp4-3692044.mp4
authors: Muskan Singh
publish: N/A

title: TIME: A Taylor-Inspired Mixed-Effects Model for IDH Prediction_supp3-3693918.pdf
authors: Pengwei Hu
publish: N/A
abstract: Background: Intradialytic hypotension (IDH) is a critical complication in hemodialysis that increases morbidity and treatment risks, yet existing machine learning methods inadequately address session-level physiological state heterogeneity and fail to balance linear and nonlinear feature interactions, limiting predictive accuracy and interpretability. Methods: We developed TIME (Taylor-Inspired Mixed-Effects Model), which explicitly separates linear interactions, higher-order nonlinear dependencies, and residual terms inspired by Taylor series expansion, while incorporating a physiological state embedding layer to capture session-level physiological state heterogeneity revealed through hierarchical clustering on autoencoder-derived latent representations of hematologic indicators selected using SHAP analysis and clinical knowledge. Using data from 532 hemodialysis patients (18,309 records), we compared TIME against 19 state-of-the-art baseline models, including blood pressure-stratified subcohort and out-of-distribution analyses. Results: Hierarchical clustering identified two distinct hematological states with different IDH risks (57.93% vs. 55.75%, $p=6.60\times 10^{-7}$). TIME achieved 0.6929 accuracy, 0.7435 F1-score, and 0.3663 MCC on the full cohort, the highest AUC across all three subcohorts (0.7010, 0.6795, and 0.7201), and strong out-of-distribution performance (accuracy 0.7053, F1 0.7377, MCC 0.4009). Integrating TIME's architecture into baseline models improved MCC by 2.67 percentage points on average. Conclusions: TIME effectively predicts IDH by modeling session-level physiological state heterogeneity and balancing feature interactions, with robust generalizability across diverse populations. Significance: TIME advances precision medicine in dialysis care by enabling early risk identification and personalized treatment strategies, while revealing broader immune, nutritional, and fluid-related markers of IDH risk.

title: Tackling the Kidnapped Robot Problem via Sparse Feasible Hypothesis Sampling and Reliable Batched Multi-Stage Inference
authors: Muhua Zhang, Lei Ma, Ying Wu, Kai Shen, Deqing Huang, Henry Leung
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper addresses the Kidnapped Robot Problem (KRP), a core localization challenge of relocalizing a robot in a known map without a prior pose estimate upon localization loss or at SLAM initialization. For this purpose, a passive 2-D global relocalization framework is proposed. It estimates the global pose efficiently and reliably from a single LiDAR scan and an occupancy grid map while the robot remains stationary, thereby enhancing the long-term autonomy of mobile robots. The proposed framework casts global relocalization as a non-convex problem and solves it using a multi-hypothesis scheme with batched multi-stage inference and early termination, balancing completeness and efficiency. The traversability-constrained Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) asymptotically covers the reachable space and restricts the search space to traversable regions in the occupancy grid, generating sparse and uniformly distributed feasible positional hypotheses. The hypotheses are preliminarily ordered by the proposed Scan Mean Absolute Difference (SMAD), a coarse beam-error-level metric that enables efficient early termination by prioritizing high-likelihood candidates and is optimized for limited scan measurements. The Translation-Affinity Scan-to-Map Alignment Metric (TAM) is introduced for reliable orientation selection and accurate final pose evaluation, mitigating the degradation of conventional likelihood-field-based metrics under translational uncertainty, non-panoramic LiDAR scans, and environmental changes. Real-world experiments on a resource-constrained mobile robot with non-panoramic LiDAR scans in two representative indoor scenarios show that the proposed framework improves the mean relocalization success rate by about 26.9 percentage points over the strongest baseline, while reducing runtime from several seconds or even tens of seconds to the sub-second to 1.6s range in most comparable cases. These results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves higher success rates with substantially lower runtime under the same time constraint, validating its effectiveness as a practical relocalization module for laser SLAM.

title: Knowledge Diffusion-Based Adaptive Alignment with Hierarchical Context for Video Temporal Grounding
authors: Ran Ran, Jiwei Wei, Yang Yang, Guoqing Wang, Kuien Liu, Jie Zou, Heng Tao Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) aims to localize the temporal moment that corresponds to a natural-language query in an untrimmed video. However, VTG faces two core challenges: narrowing the semantic gap between concise text queries and complex visual content, and extracting discriminative moment cues from long background-dominant sequences. To address these challenges, we propose Knowledge Diffusion Alignment (KDA), a diffusion-based framework that operates in the semantic feature domain. At its core, a Background Residual Diffusion Model (BRDM) starts from adaptive global video knowledge and, under query conditioning, progressively removes query-irrelevant background residuals to synthesize moment knowledge. In addition, to provide a strong conditional prior and multi-scale supervision, Hierarchical Video Knowledge Extraction (HVKE) aggregates context at multiple temporal granularities, producing both video and moment knowledge. KDA is trained in three stages: first learning target-aware moment knowledge, then aligning the diffusion process to that target, and finally performing end-to-end inference using BRDM-generated knowledge. The synthesized moment knowledge is injected into knowledge prompt reasoning, where a Dynamic Moment-aware Encoder (DME) adaptively generates dynamic temporal convolution kernels conditioned on the moment knowledge, coupling efficient local modeling with global self-attention. For prediction, a Spans-Enhanced Hierarchical Decoder (SEHD) constructs a temporal pyramid and adaptively selects the most relevant initial spans to exploit intrinsic alignment cues. Experiments across public benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed KDA outperforms the state-of-the-art methods, and extensive ablations further verify the effectiveness of the module design. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Evangelion09/HKDA</uri>.

title: Avian-Scale Flapping-Wing Air Vehicle With Independent Actuation Mechanism for Enhanced Lateral Maneuverability_supp1-3688743.pdf
authors: Jae-Hung Han
publish: N/A
abstract: Birds achieve remarkable aerial maneuverability through bilateral independent control of their wings, enabling agile flight in confined spaces and at low speeds. However, implementing such independent actuation in medium-to-heavy (>400 g) avian-scale flapping-wing air vehicles (FWAVs) has remained challenging due to power limitations of servo-driven approaches or unsynchronized phases in dual motor-linkage approaches. This article presents an independent actuation mechanism with encoder-based synchronization and validates its application to heavy avian-scale FWAVs through the 940 g KRoHawk-I platform. Each wing is driven by a separate actuation unit comprising a BLDC motor, gear train, and linkage mechanism. The actuation units are capable of rotation about the pitch axis to enable asymmetric stroke plane control. The synchronization controller maintains crank angle errors within 10° for stable flight, validated through ground tests. For lateral maneuvering, asymmetric stroke plane control generates differential angles of attack and trajectories between main wings, producing rolling moments nine times as large as those generated by tail control. Flight tests demonstrate significant maneuverability enhancement compared to tail control with symmetric stroke plane: 65% reduction in distance required for 90° turns and 70% reduction in roll response time.

title: Wavelet-enhanced Foundation Model Adaptation for Weakly Supervised Urban Tree Canopy Mapping_supp1-3693826.pdf
authors: Jingyu Yang
publish: N/A
abstract: Most existing weakly supervised methods for tree canopy segmentation suffer from limited accuracy due to insufficient supervision and poor generalization. We propose AMP—an end-to-end semantic segmentation framework that adaptively refines a pre-trained vision foundation model (DINOv2) via a lightweight CNN-based adapter. AMP comprises two key components: (1) a backbone that integrates high-frequency wavelet features with the foundation model through a novel High-Frequency Feature Integration (HFFI) module, and (2) a multi-branch pseudo-label generation module that produces diverse supervisory signals under sparse annotation. To mitigate the domain gap between DINOv2’s pretraining data and our remote sensing imagery, we design an adaptive adapter that fine-tunes the foundation model in a task-aware manner. Furthermore, to ensure pseudo-label reliability, we employ three parallel generation branches followed by a pixel-wise selection strategy to synthesize high-quality supervision. We have constructed a sparsely labeled dataset to enable and evaluate weakly supervised tree canopy segmentation in urban environments. Extensive results demonstrate that AMP achieves state-of-the-art performance under sparse supervision, significantly outperforming existing weakly supervised approaches. Which achieved an mIoU of 81.57% and an mF1 score of 89.43% on the UTC-sparse dataset. The code and data will be made publicly available at: https://github.com/Tengmingn/AMP.

title: An Air-Jet Continuum Robot for Industrial Integrated Fluidic Circuit Inspection_supp9-3682403.mp4
authors: Yongchang Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Industrial fluidic circuits operating under long-term extreme conditions are prone to defects such as cavitation, corrosion, and cracks, raising a critical demand for in-situ robotic inspection. Traditional manual disassembly-based methods are inefficient, while existing robots lack the ability to simultaneously adapt to complex geometries and maintain stable adsorption on surfaces of varying curvature. This study proposes an air-jet continuum robot (AJCR) that integrates three core modules: air-jet modules, a unit flexible continuum skeleton (UFCS), and stiffness-adjustable flexible suction cups (FSCs), enabling effective crawling and inspection inside integrated industrial fluid circuits. The UFCS provides omnidirectional bending capability of up to 196° enabling navigation through complex channels including abrupt turns of 120° inside stop valves. Under an input pressure of 0.8 MPa, the AJCR generates a maximum air-jet counter-thrust of 4.72 N for adsorption and 4.16 N for driving, ensuring reliable locomotion on unstructured, slippery, and discontinuous surfaces. Based on the sliding suction strategy, the FSC offers a maximum static adsorption force of 35.1 N, reduces sliding friction to 2.25 N on stainless steel, and adapts to curved surfaces with radii as small as 125 mm, facilitating stable sliding adsorption even across welded seams. Experimental results demonstrate that the AJCR smoothly navigates pipeline systems, enters stop valves, mixed-flow pumps and pressure vessels, and operates in liquid-filled environments, significantly improving inspection coverage and operational safety in fluidic delivery systems.

title: Refined Identification of miRNA-Disease Associations Based on Knowledge-awareness Propagation
authors: Yuliang Cai, Zhen Tang, Wei Qiao, Guiyuan Jiang, Fei Gao, Qiang He, Jiawei Zhang, Wei Qian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small non-coding RNAs orchestrating regulatory networks through sequence-specific target recognition. Understanding miRNA-disease correlations is crucial as high-throughput sequencing data growth outpaces experimental validation, necessitating computational approaches for association discovery. Existing frameworks model miRNA-disease interactions as uniform binary relationships, overlooking semantic diversity in different association mechanisms. We propose BKAMDA (<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">M</u>iRNA-<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">D</u>isease <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">A</u>ssociations prediction <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">B</u>ased on <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">K</u>nowledge-<underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">A</u>wareness), a novel knowledge-aware model for predicting miRNA-disease associations. Unlike existing methods learning only from miRNA-disease networks, BKAMDA leverages knowledge graphs to delineate distinct association types. By simulating informational propagation within knowledge graphs across diverse miRNA-disease relationships, the model investigates latent connections across various relationship types. Comparative analysis with competitive baselines using real-world experimentally validated datasets demonstrates excellent performance across multiple metrics. Three disease case studies further confirm model accuracy and effectiveness for precision medicine applications. Our knowledge-aware approach significantly advances miRNA-disease association prediction by capturing semantic diversity in biological interactions

title: Hierarchical Diffusion for Sparse-to-Full Human Motion Reconstruction
authors: Fangyu Du, Yang Yang, Xuehao Gao, Hongye Hou, Chen Sun
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Human motion generation from sparse observations is an ill-posed problem in AR/VR, where head-mounted devices often capture only head and wrist trajectories. Prior methods usually reconstruct full-body motion in a single stage, forcing inference over a vast solution space and producing inaccurate lower-body motion, weak temporal coherence, and implausible sequences that degrade avatar embodiment. We present MAGE, a Multi-stage Avatar GEnerator based on hierarchical diffusion. Instead of predicting 22-joint motion at once, MAGE progressively refines motion from a coarse 6-part representation to full joints. Each stage injects stage-specific motion priors and uses intermediate predictions to constrain subsequent refinement, reducing ambiguity and stabilizing dynamics. Experiments on large-scale motion datasets show that MAGE improves reconstruction accuracy, temporal smoothness, and perceptual realism over state-of-the-art baselines, enabling more reliable full-body animation from minimal AR/VR sensing while preserving real-time interaction.

title: ASAP: A 28-nm Transformer Training Accelerator With Alternating Sparsity and Asymmetrical Microscaling Precision
authors: HanGyeol Mun, Jian Meng, Xiaofeng Hu, Yuan Liao, Chun-Ting Chen, Jae-sun Seo
publish: IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits
abstract: This work presents ASAP, a 28-nm transformer-training accelerator that combines N:M structured sparsity with asymmetric microscaling floating-point (MXFP) precision through a unified algorithm–hardware co-design. ASAP introduces a progressive sparsity schedule in which pruned compute resources are reassigned to increase numerical precision for important weights and activations, stabilizing optimization while reducing overall computation. A placement-aware N:M reordering mechanism aligns partial products within each processing element, minimizing shift distances, lowering toggle activity, and sustaining utilization across sparsity levels. To address the performance bottleneck of nonlinear (NL) layers, a reconfigurable pipeline single-instruction multiple-data (SIMD) engine supports various NL functions and their gradients through a scatter-network datapath, achieving up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.4\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> throughput improvement. Fabricated in 28-nm CMOS, ASAP attains 22.4 TOPS/W peak efficiency and delivers 2.02–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$5.07\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> higher end-to-end training energy efficiency than prior training processors, while preserving FP32-level accuracy on MAE ViT-B and BERT workloads. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of coordinated sparsity–precision scheduling and placement-aware dataflow for future energy-efficient transformer-training accelerators.

title: Low-Crosstalk 16-Channel Second-Order Microring WDM Filter and Application to a Wavelength-Selective Switch Unit
authors: Xing Yu, Yangming Ren, Xinyu Li, Shenghang Zhou, Houyou Lai, Zhiwei Kong, Binhao Wang, Qian Chen, Xiubao Sui, Wenfu Zhang
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: A compact second-order microring filter based on optimized dual-Bezier bends is designed and experimentally validated on a silicon-on-insulator platform. This work presents the experimentally demonstrated high-performance 16-channel dense wavelength-division multiplexing device based on dual-ring filters, featuring a channel spacing of 200 GHz. Experimental characterization reveals insertion losses ranging from 0.3 to 2.9 dB, adjacent channel crosstalks suppressed below -22.1 dB, and 3-dB bandwidths of approximately 100 GHz. To further address the critical issues of optical crosstalk induced by extinction ratio degradation at the through port of high-order microring wavelength-selective switches (WSS), a 1<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times 2\times 8\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> WSS unit is proposed and demonstrated. Compared with conventional drop-through WSS architectures, the proposed dual-drop-port WSS unit achieves crosstalk levels below -20 dB for all channels. Optical communication experiment validation is performed via 30 Gbps non-return-to-zero on-off keying (NRZ-OOK) dual-channel modulated signals. The proposed scheme exhibits superior eye diagram quality and enhanced interference immunity compared to traditional WSS unit. These results provide a robust and scalable building block for the realization of large-scale, multi-wavelength on-chip high-order microring optical switching networks and high-capacity optical interconnects. A compact second-order microring filter based on optimized dual-Bezier bends is designed and experimentally validated on a silicon-on-insulator platform. This work presents the experimentally demonstrated high-performance 16-channel dense wavelength-division multiplexing device based on dual-ring filters, featuring a channel spacing of 200 GHz. Experimental characterization reveals insertion losses ranging from 0.3 to 2.9 dB, adjacent channel crosstalks suppressed below -22.1 dB, and 3-dB bandwidths of approximately 100 GHz. To further address the critical issues of optical crosstalk induced by extinction ratio degradation at the through port of high-order microring wavelength-selective switches (WSS), a 1<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\times 2\times 8\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> WSS unit is proposed and demonstrated. Compared with conventional drop-through WSS architectures, the proposed dual-drop-port WSS unit achieves crosstalk levels below -20 dB for all channels. Optical communication experiment validation is performed via 30 Gbps non-return-to-zero on-off keying (NRZ-OOK) dual-channel modulated signals. The proposed scheme exhibits superior eye diagram quality and enhanced interference immunity compared to traditional WSS unit. These results provide a robust and scalable building block for the realization of large-scale, multi-wavelength on-chip high-order microring optical switching networks and high-capacity optical interconnects.

title: Engineering LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems: A Taxonomy of Emerging Frameworks
authors: Davide Di Ruscio, Phuong T. Nguyen, Claudio Di Sipio, Riccardo Rubei, Juri Di Rocco
publish: IEEE Software
abstract: LLM–based multi-agent systems (LMAS) are emerging as a promising paradigm, enabling specialized agents to collaborate and act autonomously across complex software engineering (SE) tasks. Yet, despite rapid progress, the field still lacks a clear conceptual foundation to guide researchers and practitioners in systematically designing, implementing, and evaluating such systems. This article introduces a taxonomy intended to provide practical guidance for integrating LMAS into software development workflows. Grounded in an analysis of prominent frameworks and scientific contributions for engineering LMAS, the taxonomy organizes the design space along five key dimensions offering a structured lens for understanding and engineering LMAS. Our analysis reveals substantial gaps in current frameworks, particularly regarding monitoring, performance evaluation, and quality assessment. These findings highlight pressing research challenges and identify concrete directions toward more reliable, transparent, and effective agentic systems for SE.

title: Accurate Protein Submitochondrial Localization Using Pre-Training and Adaptive Sampling Enhanced Deep Learning Model
authors: Ting Zhu, Yuanyuan Liu, Guosheng Han
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Mitochondria consist of four primary submitochondrial compartments: the matrix, outer membrane, inner membrane, and intermembrane space. However, current research has generated relatively limited experimental data on the intermembrane space, which constrains the comprehensive understanding of its biological functions. Furthermore, existing methods for predicting protein submitochondrial localization predominantly rely on machine learning approaches that typically require expert level biological knowledge for manual feature engineering and fail to adequately address the class imbalance issue. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an end-to-end deep learning model iPreDeepSubMito for protein submitochondrial localization.
