According to the working environment and requirements of modern intelligent explosive disposal robot, this paper designs and optimizes the motion control system of explosive disposal robot, and carries out the related circuit design and simulation. Firstly, the mechanical arm, chassis, motor and other parts of the explosive disposal robot are designed and calculated, and the strength is checked. Then, SolidWorks is used for modeling and design, and the three-dimensional modeling of the intelligent explosion-proof car is completed. The three-dimensional modeling and motion simulation are carried out by SolidWorks, which verifies the mobility of the chassis in complex terrain and the stability of the manipulator operation. Finally, the required functional circuits are designed and simulated. The test shows that the explosive disposal robot can achieve the driving speed of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> on the level road, the maximum climbing angle <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 30^{\circ}$</tex>, the climbing speed of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.5 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>, and the acceleration of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$a=1 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$</tex>. These performance indicators enable the explosive disposal robot to meet the needs of most scene applications. Through the mechanical structure design and simulation, this design provides a scheme that can turn quickly and replace the structure design independently for the intelligent explosive disposal vehicle, and provides reference value for the transformation design of intelligent robot.

title: Wearable Soft Haptic Interface for Tactile Perception of Virtual Rain and Other Environmental Particles
authors: Davide Costabile, Alberto Villani, Domenico Prattichizzo
publish: 2025 IEEE Conference on Telepresence
abstract: Rendering weather phenomena within virtual environments plays a crucial role in enhancing the realism and immersiveness of user experiences. Despite the advancements in audiovisual simulation of such phenomena, their haptic representation remains relatively underexplored. The lack of tactile feedback may result in a perceptual disconnect. To address this sensory imbalance, we have developed and validated a novel wearable haptic interface. This technology is specifically designed to deliver localised, subtle, and transient tactile stimuli, emulating the ephemeral nature of suspended or falling particles as raindrops, snowflakes and sand grains, through the use of soft, compliant materials. Moreover, we exploited a couple of devised interfaces to conduct a user study that assessed whether the haptic rendering of atmospheric particles in rainy VR scenarios improves the user experience, increasing the sense of presence.

title: Research on Composite Fault Diagnosis Strategy for Axial Field Flux-Switching Permanent Magnet Motor
authors: Ziyi Tai, Haiquan Huang, Wei Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing and Automation (IMA)
abstract: To improve the fault diagnosis efficiency and accuracy of Axial Flux Flux-Switching Permanent Magnet Motors (AFFSPMMs), a composite fault diagnosis strategy for opencircuit faults and position sensor faults is proposed. First, to address the difficulty of threshold determination in conventional open-circuit fault diagnosis methods, an improved phase-current mean-based diagnostic method is presented. Then, the rotor position and speed are estimated by using an improved slidingmode observer, and the estimated speed is compared with the sensor-based speed feedback to enable rapid diagnosis of position sensor faults. Finally, the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed composite fault diagnosis strategy in an axial-flux fluxswitching permanent magnet motor are experimentally validated. The results demonstrate that the proposed method is capable of rapidly and accurately identifying the fault type and fault location.

title: Toward Immersive Teleoperation with Assistive Force Cues to Guide Users with Obstacle and Target Information
authors: Ajithkumar Narayanan Manaparampil, Annika Schmidt, Aaron Pereira, Miguel Neves, Xuwei Wu, Benedikt Pleintinger, Harsimran Singh, Michael Panzirsch, Thomas Hulin, Neal Y. Lii
publish: 2025 IEEE Conference on Telepresence
abstract: With advances in recent years, we now have several different possibilities for robot operations in complex environments. Scaling from direct teleoperation to fully autonomous planning and execution, the user may choose their level of involvement as called for by the task at hand. Direct teleoperation requires continuous user engagement, which can result in significant physical and mental workload. On the other hand, autonomous systems are frequently unreliable in unstructured or dynamic environments. Supervised autonomy bridges the gap between the two methods by automating some tasks while keeping the human in the loop and allowing them to intervene between autonomous stages. However, there is still a lack of access for intervention and course correction during each autonomous execution. The presented research introduces an assistive teleoperation approach on top of an autonomous system, where a human teleoperator can intervene, with a haptic user interface, in the robot’s motion during autonomous task execution. Our proposed approach is divided into two stages. The first stage plans the entire trajectory using a motion planner. The second stage generates assistive force cues for the planned trajectory toward the target position while avoiding known obstacles. The robot is in full control of the task execution until the operator introduces conflicting inputs to the haptic device. This inherently allows the system to function autonomously till the operator notices an error and intervenes by providing haptic inputs.

title: Smart Hydrogen Microgrids for Powering Polar Vehicles and Drones Using IoT and Satellite-Assisted Intelligent Networks in Extreme Environments
authors: Mekha K Babu, R.Mamat, Noreffendy Tamaldin, Adrian Valentin Boicea, S. Shanmuga Priya, K. Sudhakar
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Extreme environments such as Antarctica create major challenges for generating, storing, and transporting energy. Persistent sub-zero temperatures, remote locations, and limited infrastructure make reliable power supply difficult. Because of this, many polar stations still depend on diesel generators. While these systems are familiar and relatively dependable, they are not environmentally sustainable and require complicated fuel logistics, including long-distance transport and seasonal storage. To address these issues, this study introduces a Smart Hydrogen Microgrid designed to support energy needs for polar ground vehicles and autonomous aerial drones. The concept combines renewable energy, hydrogen-based storage, and intelligent communication networks into a single integrated system. In this framework, hybrid solar and wind resources generate electricity that is used for hydrogen production through water electrolysis. This approach provides a clean method for storing energy over long periods, which is particularly valuable in remote regions with variable renewable supply. The hydrogen produced is later converted back into electricity using fuel cells. This electricity powers electric vehicles and unmanned aerial systems used for logistics, surveillance, and environmental monitoring. At the same time, an Internet of Things (IoT) sensing layer continuously tracks renewable power output, hydrogen storage levels, vehicle operation, and key environmental conditions. Since communication in polar regions is often unreliable, the system also incorporates satellite-assisted connectivity combined with edge computing. This setup allows stable data exchange and supports near real-time decision-making even when network access becomes intermittent. Within the overall architecture, intelligent control algorithms manage energy distribution, optimize vehicle routing, and maintain system performance under changing environmental and operational conditions. Overall, the proposed framework strengthens energy resilience, improves operational reliability, and reduces dependence on fossil fuel supply chains. It also aligns with current developments in smart microgrids, renewable energy integration, and intelligent energy management systems. Although designed for polar research stations, the approach can be scaled and adapted for other remote or infrastructure-limited environments.

title: Embedded Tutorial: AI Vs. Verification Engineer: Fight or Synergy?
authors: Marcela Zachariasova
publish: 2026 IEEE 29th International Symposium on Design and Diagnostics of Electronic Circuits and Systems (DDECS)
abstract: The paper investigates the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms in two critical areas of simulationbased verification: (1) the development of verification testbenches (2) debugging activities within simulation environments. The primary objective is to assess how AI can enhance the productivity of verification engineers and improve the overall efficiency and quality of verification workflows in industrial practice. The paper presents the role of functional verification in the chip development flow, reviews widely adopted verification methodologies, AI techniques already used for automation and analysis, as well as the recent state-of-the-art research activities targeting emerging verification challenges. A key contribution is the identification of a significant gap in publicly available, high-quality open-source datasets tailored specifically for training AI models in hardware verification tasks. To ground the discussion in industrial reality, the paper also presents results from a survey conducted among 50 verification engineers, providing insight into which parts of a testbench could most benefit from AI-driven code generation or AI assistance. By combining a comprehensive state-of-the-art review with empirical insights from industry practitioners, this paper provides a structured analysis of current limitations and outlines concrete future research directions aligned with realworld verification needs.

title: Smartguard: AI-driven drowsiness and health monitoring for drivers
authors: Sharmikha Sree R, Vennila M S, Keerthika A, Lalitha K
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid drowsy driver detection and health monitoring system designed to improve traffic safety through real-time fatigue assessment. The proposed system combines infrared camera-based face analysis using Haar cascades and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with wearable physiological sensing to monitor heart rate and SpO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> levels. An onboard controller combines visual and biometric data to accurately detect driver drowsiness and activate a three-tiered warning mechanism, including audible warnings, IoT-based emergency alerts and automated vehicle braking if the driver is unresponsive. A web-based interface provides real-time visualization of driver health metrics and notification status for remote monitoring. The multimodal fusion approach improves detection reliability and reduces false alarms compared to single-sensor methods, providing a cost-effective and practical solution for proactive crash prevention in long-distance and commercial driving scenarios.

title: RadTempSense: Physics-Informed Degradation Modeling with Adaptive Compensation and Multi-Modal Diagnosis for Radiation-Hardened Fiber Optic Temperature Sensors
authors: Wenlong Zhang, Wenhua Yang, Liang Zhang, Qianqian Wang, Yaohan Hu, Xiaofeng Sun
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Electronic Technology, Computers and Software Engineering (AETCSE)
abstract: Fiber optic temperature sensors deployed in nuclear reactors and high-energy physics facilities face severe degradation under ionizing radiation, compromising measurement integrity and operational safety. Current approaches suffer from three critical limitations: (1) lack of physics-informed models capturing radiation-induced attenuation (RIA) mechanisms and their coupling with thermal dynamics, (2) absence of dose-aware compensation strategies that adapt to progressive sensor degradation, and (3) inability to discriminate between multiple failure modes in radiation-damaged Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors. We propose RadTempSense, a comprehensive framework integrating radiation physics modeling, adaptive compensation, and multi-modal failure diagnosis. Our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) a coupled RIA-temperature error model incorporating color center formation, densification, and thermal annealing effects, (2) a dose-aware compensation mechanism with online calibration that reduces measurement errors by up to 78% at high radiation doses, and (3) a Dynamic Diagnostic Similarity Matrix (DDSM) for tracking failure mode evolution across radiation exposure levels. Extensive experiments on simulated nuclear-grade sensor data demonstrate that RadTempSense maintains temperature measurement accuracy within ±2°C up to 35 kGy cumulative dose, while achieving 94.3% diagnostic accuracy for five distinct failure modes.

title: Nodule Detection in Breast Ultrasound Using Deep Learning-Based Instance Segmentation
authors: R. Anusree, R. Abhilash Hareendranathan, C.P. Mohammed Suhail
publish: 2026 International Conference on Future and Advanced Computing Technologies (ICFACT)
abstract: This study assesses the effectiveness of the instance segmentation algorithm using YOLO26 and its derivatives in instance segmentation within Ultrasound imaging. It aims to evaluate their ability to accurately detect breast cancer and segment three classes, such as benign, malignant, and normal. Performance metrics such as precision, recall, mAP50, and mAP50-95 will be utilized to compare different YOLO26 variants. Additionally, the research will investigate the computational efficiency of these algorithms to gauge their suitability for potential application in breast cancer detection using ultrasound. Among the evaluated YOLO26 variants for breast ultrasound instance segmentation, YOLO26l achieved the best performance with an mAP50 of 0.643 and mAP50-95 of 0.473, demonstrating stable accuracy across multiple IoU thresholds. The YOLO26x variant showed comparable but slightly lower performance despite increased model complexity. YOLO26I also exhibited favorable inference speed, indicating a strong balance between accuracy and computational efficiency for real-time clinical ultrasound applications.

title: Rolling Optimization of a Multi-Period Two-Dimensional Cutting Stock Problem Considering the Future Value of the Remnant Materials
authors: Qiong Xia, Yuzun Wang, Siyuan Yue, Ao Li, Shihao Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing and Automation (IMA)
abstract: A predictive rolling 2D cutting method is put forward to consider extra future value when orders are placed at different times. Each window solves a bi-objective subproblem that minimizes the present procurement cost and maximizes the end-of-period surplus value, where future value includes both static reusability and dynamic reuse probability (Beta-binomial with forgetting), adjusted by a reset index; decoding is done using a priority index to weigh current savings against waiting value. Scenario layering + multiple seeds experiments show <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim \text{1 3. 7 {\%}}$</tex> net cost cut, more reuse, less expiring, and 2 nd level runtime per period, showing its solid and can be engineered.

title: Lateral and Longitudinal Position Estimation and Control for Dynamic WPT Using Receiver Current and Vehicle Dynamics Information_supp1-3694019.mp4
authors: Osamu Shimizu
publish: N/A
abstract: Dynamic wireless power transfer (DWPT) promises to extend the driving range of electric vehicles. However, efficiency is severely reduced by coil misalignment. Especially when decelerating and stopping in urban driving, both longitudinal and lateral positions become critical. Achieving precise alignment without costly dedicated WPT sensors remains challenging because roadembedded coils are invisible. To address this, this article proposes an integrated position estimation and control framework with a simple current sensor and other vehicle motion sensors. An Unscented Kalman Filter simultaneously estimates both lateral and longitudinal positions by fusing receiver side DC current and vehicle dynamics with a pre-measured mutual inductance map, eliminating the need for dedicated WPT sensors. To ensure accurate alignment, a feedback controller is developed utilizing the estimated states. Experiments using a full-scale electric vehicle, which achieves a maximum received electric power of approximately 1.5 kW, demonstrate that the proposed method achieved a stopping accuracy of 31.4mm and 13.9mm in Root Mean Square Error in the longitudinal and lateral directions, respectively. This precise alignment improves energy efficiency by approximately 6.7% compared to a lateral misalignment of 60mm. Furthermore, through benchmarking with standard Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) functions, specifically Lane Keeping Assist and Adaptive Cruise Control, the results verify that the proposed framework possesses the potential to effectively augment the ADAS system to achieve the precise alignment essential for efficient DWPT.

title: Transient Nonlinear-Loss Responses of Ferrite Microstrip Structures to High-Power Microwave Pulses Under Perpendicular-Pumping Configuration
authors: Mingyu Yang, Haiyang Wang, Fadhel M. Ghannouchi, Cheng Zhang, Huan Yan, Jianing Zhao, Hao Li, Qiwei Li, Lianghao Guo, Tianming Li, Zhenyu Zhou, Sidi Liu, Yiyun Wang, Shiyong Ji, Biao Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: A theoretical and experimental investigation of the threshold and loss responses of nonlinear (NL) loss effects in ferrite microstrip structures excited by high-power microwave (HPM) pulses under a perpendicular-pumping configuration is studied. Based on the coupling dynamics between the uniform precession mode and parametrically excited half-frequency spin-wave pairs, a pulse duration <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\tau $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-dependent critical threshold model <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$h_{c}^{\bot }(\tau)$</tex-math> </inline-formula> is developed. In addition, an equivalent simulation method for NL power loss under above-threshold excitation is developed in polycrystalline yttrium iron garnet (YIG) substrate. This method enables the power dissipation along the transmission path and the evolution of the radio frequency (RF) magnetic field to be evaluated in commercial full-wave electromagnetic simulators. Experimental and simulation results at 9.6GHz indicate that, when <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\tau $</tex-math> </inline-formula> is shorter than 100ns, the threshold power exhibits an approximate inverse dependence on <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\tau $</tex-math> </inline-formula>, consistent with the short-pulse energy-threshold behavior expected from classical transient parametric-excitation theory. For a given incident power, a higher threshold leads to weaker achievable NL loss. Increasing the YIG substrate thickness reduces the internal RF magnetic-field intensity, thereby significantly increasing the threshold power and weakening the loss capability. Specifically, for <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\tau = 200$</tex-math> </inline-formula>ns, the threshold power of a 0.5-mm-thick sample is about 14.5times larger than that of a 0.3-mm-thick sample, while the NL loss at an incident power of approximately 600W is reduced by about 5dB. The good agreement between simulations and measurements demonstrates that the proposed method can quantitatively characterize transient NL loss behavior of ferrites under a perpendicular-pumping configuration.

title: Energy-Efficient Cable-Driven Robot Actuator With an Eccentric Multisingular Mechanism
authors: Seungbum Lim, Seungcheol Oh, Jungwook Suh
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Energy efficiency has become increasingly important in robotic systems due to rising energy consumption and environmental concerns. To address this issue, this study presents a low-power tug-of-war cable-driven actuator that reduces drive motor torque demand by providing assistive torque through spring pretension. The proposed actuator adopts a hybrid architecture in which a drive motor generates joint motion, while an assist motor regulates spring pretension. To eliminate the continuous power normally required to maintain high pretension, an eccentric multisingular mechanism is introduced at the assist motor. Owing to its geometric characteristics, the eccentric spool creates specific rotational configurations where the effective moment arm approaches zero, enabling zero-power holding under cable tension. For experimental validation, the proposed actuator was implemented on a three-degree-of-freedom cable-driven manipulator and evaluated through assistive-force characterization, zero-power holding verification, repetitive trajectory tracking, and break-even analysis under different external load conditions. Experimental results show that the proposed actuator reduces drive motor current and total energy consumption by more than 50% compared with a nonassisted configuration, while the switching cost of pretension control is recovered within a small number of cycles. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed actuator for energy-efficient cable-driven robotic systems.

title: MuSCAT:An End-to-end Multi-scale Confidence-Aware Deep Framework for Understanding Text from Images in Unconstrained Environments
authors: Aditya Patil, Tauseef Khan, Ayatullah Faruk Mollah
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Understanding texts from imagery is pertinent for diverse applications ranging from traditional text-embedded image reading to perceiving text-labeled environments. However, text reading in the wild is quite challenging due to environmental heterogeneity, degradation and deformation of acquired images, multi-lingual scenarios, diverse text appearance, etc. Though recent deep learning approaches are demonstrating reasonably good performance, they often struggle in complex and wild imagery. In this paper, a novel multi-scale end-to-end deep framework is presented for effective text understanding from complex and multilingual scene images. It involves (i) crafting of fuzzy textness map with VGG-16 driven class-confidence aggregation of multi-scale text proposals, (ii) designing of a soft-to-hard tuneable tightness-aware text detection technique using the fuzzy textness map, and (iii) recognition of the detected text regions followed by translation into native language. Rigorous experiments over a number of related benchmark datasets viz. ICDAR 2013 focused scene text, AU-MSTR and Total-Text and obtained findings demonstrate high accuracy in text extraction, recognition and translation in uncontrollable environments, outperforming other contemporary methods. It has produced an overall f-measure score of 85.39% on the AU-MSTR, 86.03% on the ICDAR 2013 and 81.69% on the Total-Text, indicating its effectiveness and robustness. The source codes and documentation have been publicly released at https://github.com/adinp1213/MuSCATText-Reader.

title: Efficient Decentralized Federated Generative Adversarial Network Learning over Multi-Source Heterogeneous Data_supp1-3693874.pdf
authors: Alia Asheralieva
publish: N/A
abstract: We propose DHetGAN, a decentralized federated generative adversarial network (GAN) learning framework for multi-source heterogeneous data. Unlike standard decentralized federated learning for non-GAN models, DHetGAN requires each node to solve a local minimax GAN game via adversarial training of its discriminator and generator, which are then averaged in a decentralized manner to reach consensus. To address data heterogeneity across nodes, we develop a GAN-intrinsic aggregation strategy that assigns averaging weights using a privacy-preserving divergence proxy quantifying inter-node distributional divergence. The proxy is estimated locally by each node – without revealing sensitive data – from coupled discriminator-genera tor values calibrated by the neighborhood-level maximal discrimina tor confidence, specific to the minimax GAN game. To reduce communication overhead, we present a compression method tailored to DHet GAN. We theoretically analyze convergence guarantees of our frame work, demonstrating strong inter-node coupling, contracting gradient magnitudes, and uniformly bounded consensus dynamics for both discriminators and generators. We also establish a sufficient condition for full mode coverage in DHetGAN and identify practical training set tings under which this condition holds. Extensive experiments and comparisons with benchmarks show that DHetGAN achieves superior generative performance, strong stability, diversity of mode coverage, and fast convergence, while substantially reducing communication overhead, even under extreme non-i.i.d. data heterogeneity.

title: Semantic-Oriented Robust Sentence Level Text Watermark for Large Language Models
authors: Bo Li, Kun Zhang, Cheng Song, Xi Chen, Hailin Zhou, Richang Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Text watermarking focuses on embedding identifiable information into the generated content, which has become increasingly important with the rapid development of large language models (LLMs). Existing watermarking works either divide the vocabulary of LLMs into “green” and “red” tokens for the watermark generation (i.e., token-level watermark), or use the distance of generated sentence embeddings to distinguish the “green” and “red” partitions (i.e., sentence-level watermark). Despite the achieved progress, existing methods remain vulnerable to various attacks or exhibit limited generalization to out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. To this end, we focus on sentence-level watermarking and propose a novel semantic-oriented robust text watermark for LLMs (SoTW). Specifically, we first employ a pre-trained embedding model to extract representations of generated sentences. Then, different from existing sentence-level works, we design a novel semantic quantization autoencoder (SQAE) to generate discrete sentence partition representations. Moreover, a semantic loss and a consistency loss are designed to ensure the generalization and robustness of generated watermarks. Furthermore, we develop an efficient detection method for our proposed SoTW. Extensive experiments on three LLMs across three publicly available datasets demonstrate the robustness of SoTW against various attacks in OOD settings. We also release the code to facilitate the community<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/libolb/SoTW</uri>.

title: ARCHON: An Agentic AI Approach for Self-Optimized and Adaptive Intent Management in Distributed Computation Over 6G
authors: Petros Maratos, Eleni Fotopoulou, Anastasios Zafeiropoulos, Constantinos Vassilakis, Grigorios Kakkavas, Symeon Papavassiliou
publish: IEEE Network
abstract: A paradigm shift is underway in 6G from the design of reactive systems to autonomous and adaptive solutions that take advantage of agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI). Agentic AI solutions emerge to inject distributed intelligence into 6G management and orchestration, where multiple agents can plan steps and collaborate to achieve common goals. In the current article, we detail an Agentic AI approach for managing the intent for distributed applications deployed over 6G infrastructure. Distributed applications are provided on computing resources that span from the radio access to the edge to the core part of a 6G-enabled computing continuum. The proposed approach leverages Knowledge Graph and Generative AI technologies, enabling agents to reason over explicit relationships and dynamically generate explainable and reliable plans. A prototype implementation is detailed, accompanied by evaluation results that highlight the efficiency of the proposed approach to improve automation and efficiency on the intent lifecycle management.

title: Small Traffic Object Detection on Drone Views: A Lightweight Network Based on Neural Architecture Search and Multi-Scale Feature Enhancement
authors: Wei Sun, Xuancheng Liu, Xiaorui Zhang, Tiantian Peng, Quanbo Ge
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: In the field of intelligent transportation, the integration of drones with object detection algorithms enables the flexible collection of traffic information. To address the challenges posed by limited computational resources on drone platforms and the small object sizes observed from aerial views, this study proposes a lightweight detection network for small traffic objects. Specifically, a new neural architecture search (NAS) method based on the principle of maximum entropy is introduced. By considering actual latency as a constraint, we employ a coarse-to-fine evolutionary search strategy to develop a lightweight and high-performance backbone network. Meanwhile, the model’s accuracy and real-time performance are further enhanced by incorporating a dual-branch anchor-free decoupled head that effectively separates the tasks of classification and localization. To tackle the issue of detecting small objects in aerial images, a cascade receptive field aggregation module (CRAM) is proposed, which embeds receptive field paths of different sizes into the feature pyramid, thereby enhancing the model’s multi-scale feature learning capability. Furthermore, a new Wise-IoU loss with distance attention is employed to more accurately regress ground truth boxes of small traffic objects and improves detection accuracy. Extensive experiments on the VisDrone2019-DET dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The proposed method shows great potential for enabling the implementation of traffic object detection algorithms on resource-constrained devices such as drones.

title: Decision Transformers for RIS-Assisted Systems with Diffusion Model-Based Channel Acquisition
authors: Jie Zhang, Yiyang Ni, Jun Li, Guangji Chen, Zhe Wang, Long Shi, Shi Jin, Wen Chen, H. Vincent Poor
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) have been recognized as a revolutionary technology for future wireless networks. However, RIS-assisted communications have to continuously tune phase-shifts relying on accurate channel state information (CSI) that is generally difficult to obtain due to the large number of RIS channels. The joint design of CSI acquisition and subsection RIS phase-shifts remains a significant challenge in dynamic environments. In this paper, we propose a diffusion-enhanced decision Transformer (DEDT) framework consisting of a diffusion model (DM) designed for efficient CSI acquisition and a decision Transformer (DT) utilized for phase-shift optimizations. Specifically, we first propose a novel DM mechanism, i.e., conditional imputation based on denoising diffusion probabilistic model, for rapidly acquiring real-time full CSI by exploiting the spatial correlations inherent in wireless channels. Then, we optimize beamforming schemes based on the DT architecture, which pre-trains on historical environments to establish a robust policy model. Next, we incorporate a fine-tuning mechanism to ensure rapid beamforming adaptation to new environments, eliminating the retraining process that is imperative in conventional reinforcement learning (RL) methods. Simulation results demonstrate that DEDT can enhance efficiency and adaptability of RIS-aided communications with fluctuating channel conditions compared to state-of-the-art RL methods.

title: Pinched Antenna Systems for Sustainable Net-Zero 6G Near-Field Wireless Communications
authors: Arnav Mukhopadhyay, Keshav Singh, Fan-Shuo Tseng, Kapal Dev, Hyundong Shin
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: Current sixth-generation (6G) antenna schemes risk falling short of net-zero emission goals, as they often require large numbers of active radio-frequency (RF) elements and substantial material resources in order to deliver both high-capacity connectivity and precise near-field sensing. There remains a critical gap: architectures that can meet next-generation connectivity demands while sharply reducing energy consumption, carbon emissions, and environmental impact. This article addresses this gap by placing pinched antenna systems (PASS) at the center of a human-centric, energy- and carbon-aware 6G vision. PASS employs movable radiators on low-loss dielectric guides, creating scalable and reconfigurable apertures controlled via digital precoding, per-guide analog weighting, and dynamic pinching. This tri-hybrid approach enables energy-proportional operation, sharp near-field focusing, and significant reductions in active RF hardware and spillover, directly supporting IMT-2030 and sustainable development goals (SDG)-aligned goals. System-level studies show that right-sizing the PASS aperture yields efficiency gains that plateau, guiding optimal resource allocation and minimizing material use. By clustering pinching elements near active users, PASS further reduces interference and unnecessary power expenditure. The architecture enables integrated sensing, wireless power transfer, and physical-layer security, while its modular design supports circularity, repair, and carbon-aware control. Open challenges include scalable calibration, full-duplex operation, and integration with other 6G antenna paradigms. Collectively, PASS emerges as a practical and sustainable foundation for future wireless networks.

title: Dual-Beam Sweeping and Tracking for mmWave Massive MIMO ISAC Using Hierarchical Codebooks
authors: Hang Yu, Jianxiao Xie, Zejun Zhang, Ziyu Yan, QingLong Fei
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: Efficient beam alignment for millimeter-wave (mmWave) massive MIMO integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) must jointly address communication–sensing resource coupling and user/target mobility. This letter proposes a weighted hierarchical dual-beam sweeping and tracking framework, where candidate communication/sensing beam pairs are evaluated by a coupled score with a power-split factor ρ, enabling a controllable signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) tradeoff. To improve coarse-layer reliability, aperture-preserving hierarchical codebooks are adopted to synthesize wide beams by coherently combining full-aperture narrow beams with an offline phase taper. To enhance temporal robustness, tracker-assisted refinement based on cubature/extended Kalman filtering or spline extrapolation is further combined with a rollback mechanism to suppress abnormal estimation jumps. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves higher angular accuracy, a smoother communication–sensing tradeoff, and lower error-spike and estimate-jump rates than representative benchmark methods, demonstrating better robustness and stability.

title: HSANet: Hierarchical Semantic Alignment Network via Local-Global Adaptive Injection for Cropland Extraction
authors: Bo Zhang, Guoyou Li, Qiang Zhang, Yaxiong Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Delineation of farmland parcels is essential for precision agriculture and environmental monitoring, yet remains challenging due to spectral variability, boundary ambiguity, and complex background interference. We present HSANet, a high-precision semantic segmentation network built on three synergistic innovations. First, the Local-Global Adaptive Injection (LGAI) Module fuses local and global features via dual paths: a local branch with the MogaMambaEnhancer (MME) captures fine-grained textures through gated bottleneck and bidirectional Mamba scanning, while a global branch with the GlobalMambaKernel (GMK) models long-range dependencies via visual state-space modeling and decomposed large-kernel convolution. Second, the Global Pyramid Enhancer (GPE) Head replaces conventional refinement convolutions in the feature pyramid with a global convolution interaction mechanism, jointly enhancing multi-scale representations with global context and local detail. Third, the Anisotropic Error Diffusion and Structural Consolidation Loss (AEDSLoss) identifies structurally significant errors through iterative diffusion and applies modulated focal and geometric penalties to enforce pixel-level accuracy and topological coherence. Extensive experiments on the FGFD and FTW datasets demonstrate that HSANet consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, achieving mIoU/F1 of 87.25%/93.19% and 72.26%/83.90%, respectively, highlighting its potential for high-resolution agricultural mapping.

title:
Overcoming BEOL Thermal Constraints in Heterogenous Integration of α-Ga
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    UV-C Photodetectors Using Low-Temperature Thermal Compression Bonding
authors: Ji Yun Lee, Dong Yun Sung, Sun Jae Kim, Jong Kyung Park, Sarah Eunkyung Kim, Dae-Woo Jeon, Wan SiK Hwang, Seul Ki Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: This work demonstrates a low-temperature heterogeneous integration strategy for α-Ga₂O₃-based functional devices that complies with the stringent thermal budget constraints (<400 °C) of silicon CMOS back-end-of-line (BEOL) processing. Although α-Ga₂O₃ exhibits excellent solar-blind UV-C photoresponse, its high epitaxial growth temperature (>450 °C) has hindered direct monolithic integration with CMOS circuitry. To address this incompatibility, α-Ga₂O₃ was synthesized at 470 °C and subsequently integrated at 350 °C using a Cu–Cu thermal compression bonding (TCB) platform. A dual-functional Ti interlayer architecture, consisting of a 10 nm passivation layer and a 50 nm adhesion layer, facilitates low-temperature bonding while maintaining interfacial stability between metal and oxide layers. Cu–Cu TCB performed at 350 °C under an applied pressure of 10 MPa resulted in a continuous and void-free metallurgical junction. Atomic-scale interfacial analyses indicate that the 50 nm Ti adhesion layer plays a key role in limiting Cu diffusion and mitigating thermo-mechanical stress, contributing to the preservation of the α-Ga₂O₃ active layer during bonding. The heterogeneously integrated UV-C photodetector exhibits photo-to-dark current ratios comparable to those of devices fabricated under high-temperature growth conditions, even under ultra-low-intensity UV-C illumination. These results demonstrate that low-temperature Cu–Cu TCB can be extended beyond conventional BEOL-compatible packaging to enable the integration of high-temperature functional oxides within BEOL thermal constraints. This work highlights a packaging-level heterogeneous integration strategy rather than device-level performance optimization.

title: Torque-based Electromechanical Coupling Analysis for Railway Vibration Energy Harvesting System
authors: Siyuan Wu, Bin Zhang, Xingwei Wei, Jie Luo, Pavel M. Trivailo, Jun Gao, Shengxi Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Energy Conversion
abstract: Vibration energy generated during train operations can be harvested to provide a stable power supply for distributed wireless sensor nodes. Existing research has primarily focused on optimizing energy harvesting structures and interface circuits, whereas mechanical damage under prolonged vibration exposure remains underexplored. This study invests the dynamics of the energy harvester to clarify how excitation forces influence structural stability. The irregular nature of track vibrations makes generator torque the primary means of controlling excitation force variations. Therefore, a torque prediction model was established by integrating the operating principles of buck converters. This model accurately forecasts dynamic torque changes based on load variations, thereby enabling the assessment of structural damage induced by load fluctuations. Experimental results show that at 100 r/min and load resistances from 3Ω to 4kΩ, model prediction errors remain below 5%. Additionally, comparative analysis with two types of DC-DC converter validates the model dynamic behavior. Reducing current and adopting high-efficiency converters both contribute to torque optimization. A low-torque current-limiting (LTCL) circuit is designed to mitigate high-torque durations and further validate the accuracy of the proposed model.

title: MITLIR: An Imitation-based Reward Shaping Method in Autonomous Driving Scenarios through Map in the Loop
authors: Shan He, Shen Ying, Shi Chen, Yang Zhang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) possesses substantial decision-making potential, yet its advantages over imitation learning (IL) in autonomous driving are not immediately evident, primarily due to the absence of human prior knowledge, which often leads to cold-start problems. To address this, the human-in-the-loop (HITL) approach has emerged, incorporating human experts into the agent training process to facilitate policy convergence. However, traditional HITL methods rely heavily on real-time human supervision and ambiguity in human-designed reward functions poses a significant limitation to the broader application of RL. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a novel map-in-the-loop imitation-based reward shaping method (MITLIR). In this approach, a high-definition (HD) map-based navigation algorithm serves as the expert policy, thereby reducing human labor costs. Drawing inspiration from imitation learning, the expert actions are used to define a loss between the expert and agent actions, which in turn is adopted as the reward signal in reinforcement learning. The main contributions are as follows: (1) This form of reward shaping only requires explicit low-level action outputs from the expert, without relying on semantic interpretations derived from human experience, thus enabling real-time, gradient-rich feedback for the agent. (2) It encourages the agent to mimic the expert policy and effectively mitigates the RL cold-start problem through HITL action interventions. (3) we examine its adaptability to exploratory policies and its integration with sparse rewards, as well as its compatibility with fine-tuning in continued training, (4) We demonstrate its robustness compared to interactive imitation learning algorithms using the same policy network and loss function under noisy conditions.

title: Interpreting Short Social Media Videos with Multimodal LLMs: A Validation Experiment
authors: Sandy Liu, Ella Haig, Eni Mustafaraj
publish: Proceedings of the 18th ACM Web Science Conference 2026
abstract: None

title: A Saturated Cross-Forming Control for Stability Enhancement of Grid-Forming Inverters During Grid Fault Recovery
authors: Jialun Yang, Hua Ye, Qianying Mou, Xiuqiang He, Yutian Liu, Hua Geng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Grid-forming (GFM) inverters with an overcurrent limiting strategy called cross-forming control published in recent literature are endowed with both voltage angle-forming and current magnitude-forming capabilities, which ensure inertia support and precise overcurrent limiting during faults. However, the risk of losing stability caused by positive feedback in the cross-forming control during grid fault recovery is found in this paper. To understand and address this problem, this paper first analytically derives the operating ranges of constant voltage control (CVC) and current limiting control (CLC) in terms of power angle. Then, an unstable region induced by the loss of operating points is identified within the operating range of CLC. Subsequently, a saturated cross-forming control method is proposed to completely eliminate the unstable region. The proposed method guarantees global fault recovery capability of GFM inverters without compromising the original operating region of cross-forming control.

title: An Integrated Power Generation Center with an Internal Variable- Voltage DC Bus for More Electric Aircraft Application
authors: Xiaoyu Lang, Tao Yang, Hengliang Zhang, Wei Hua, Serhiy Bozhko, Patrick Wheeler
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: This letter proposes an integrated power generation center and its associated control scheme for dualspool turbofan-based more-electric aircraft. The architecture introduces an additional variable-voltage dc bus, which addresses two critical limitations: firstly, it removes the efficiency loss caused by field-weakening operation in the conventional high-pressure spool generator (HPG) at high speeds; secondly, it enables high-power resistive loads, such as electrical wing ice-protection systems (eWIPS), to be directly connected to a variable-voltage dc bus without an additional dc/dc converter. Besides these two contributions, the proposed control scheme also incorporates power control of the back-toback (BTB) converter and reactive power compensation based on the instantaneous power theory. The letter illustrates this innovative architecture and presents a coordinated control strategy that simultaneously achieves field-weakening-free HPG operation, flexible power regulation for the eWIPS and stable BTB converter power control.

title: TSA-Former: Linear Transformer with Taylor Series Attention for Sensor-Based Human Activity Recognition
authors: Qifan Sun, Zechen Li, Kun Wang, Zenan Fu, Guangjie Chen, Lei Zhang, Hao Wu, Aiguo Song
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Transformer models have demonstrated superior capability in capturing long-range temporal dependencies crucial for Sensor-Based Human Activity Recognition (HAR). However, the quadratic computational complexity inherent to the Softmax-Attention mechanism significantly impedes their deployment on resource-constrained wearable devices and real-time streaming tasks. To address this, we propose a novel Linear Transformer with Taylor Series Attention specifically tailored for the HAR domain, named TSA-Former. It leverages the first-order Taylor expansion to approximate the Softmax-Attention and utilizes the norm-preserving mapping to approximate the high-order non-linear information, resulting in a linear computational complexity. In addition, TSA-Former integrates a multi-branch architecture featuring multi-scale patch embedding, which enables the model to dynamically capture multi-scale temporal features while minimizing overhead. Experimental results across four public HAR benchmarks, namely UniMiB-SHAR, UCI-HAR, WISDM, and OPPORTUNITY, demonstrate that TSA-Former achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) accuracy and efficiency, outperforming conventional Transformers and existing linear-attention models. Deployment experiments conducted on the Raspberry Pi 5 platform further validate the model’s superior low-latency and minimal power consumption profile, confirming its robust suitability for real-world embedded HAR applications. Code will be released.

title: Optical Fiber Intelligent Carpet for Gait Recognition with A Local Strain and Global Contour Dual-modality Attention Network
authors: Xiang Li, Mingcong Sun, Zhaoyang Wang, Bing Han, Yanqiong Wang, Zining Wang, Yong Zhao, Ting Feng
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Gait recognition is an important technology to perceive human motion patterns, which is widely used in intelligent medical, security monitoring and human-computer interaction. Traditional gait recognition methods mostly rely on image analysis or wearable sensors, which are restricted by environmental conditions and difficult to balance accuracy and convenience. This paper constructs an intelligent carpet gait recognition system using Optical Frequency Domain Reflectometry (OFDR) based distributed fiber optic sensing. By embedding two-dimensional optical fiber array inside the carpet, it achieves high-density perception of strains of the foot. A local strain and global contour dual-modality attention network is proposed for gait recognition. Firstly, a strain graph attention network is designed to process the original strain data, which can construct the dynamic correlation between fibers and extract key fiber strain features. Then, the strain signal is transformed into image and a pressure gradient guided non-local attention module is introduced to focus on the contact area and contour of the sole, so as to enhance the global spatial features of the gait. Finally, the dual-modality features are fused to realize gait recognition. The proposed method is tested on 16 kinds of gaits. The quantitative and qualitative experiment results show that the proposed method can effectively collect gait data and has excellent recognition accuracy.

title: Real-Time Pedestrian Detection and Multi-Object Tracking in Complex Airport Scenes via Frequency-Aware and Mamba-Enhanced Representation
authors: Can Zhou, Kehua Liu, Kefei Hu, Hai Wang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Real-time pedestrian detection and multi-object tracking (MOT) in complex airport surveillance scenarios present significant challenges, including drastic scale variation, frequent occlusion, cluttered backgrounds, and diverse motion dynamics. Existing methods often fail to simultaneously satisfy accuracy, robustness, and real-time requirements in such demanding environments. To address these challenges, we propose AirMOTR, a unified detection-and-tracking framework tailored for airport-scene pedestrian perception. First, we integrate a lightweight two-dimensional Selective State Space Model (SS2D) into the backbone feature extractor to efficiently capture long-range spatial dependencies while maintaining low computational overhead. Second, we introduce three complementary feature enhancement modules: a Frequency-aware Interaction Block (FIB) that employs discrete wavelet transform to enrich feature representations in the frequency domain; a Query-driven Context Extractor (QCE) that aggregates contextual semantics through detection-query guidance; and an Adaptive Spatial-Channel Gate (ASCG) that dynamically modulates feature responses across both spatial and channel dimensions. Third, we embed the Motion Mamba (MM) module within MM-Tracker, combining local correlation and global bidirectional scanning to generate accurate inter-frame motion maps. A novel Motion Margin Loss (MMLoss) is also proposed to adaptively adjust association boundaries based on per-target motion magnitude, improving tracking robustness for both slow-moving and fast-moving airport passengers. For training, we first pre-train on the large-scale CrowdHuman and WiderPedestrian datasets to strengthen pedestrian feature generalization, then fine-tune on our newly constructed AirportPassenger dataset and validate generalizability on the ASS (Airport Security Surveillance) dataset. Notably, SS2D andMMform a complementary pair: SS2D captures global spatial dependencies within individual frames, while MM leverages bidirectional scanning across temporal frames for motion modeling, jointly addressing the spatial and temporal challenges of airport surveillance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AirMOTR outperforms state-of-the-art methods on all benchmarks while maintaining real-time inference speed (≈41.6 FPS), providing a robust and practical solution for intelligent airport surveillance. Code is available at https://github.com/zhoucancqa/AirMOTR.

title: Genetic Programming Based Surrogate Modeling for Synthesis of Printed Ridge Gap Waveguide Unit Cells
authors: Mohammed Farouk Nakmouche, Dominic Deslandes, Ghyslain Gagnon
publish: IEEE Journal on Multiscale and Multiphysics Computational Techniques
abstract: This paper introduces, for the first time, a scalable and interpretable genetic programming (GP)-based surrogate modeling approach within an explainable AI framework for the synthesis of Printed Ridge Gap Waveguide (PRGW) unit cells. The proposed approach targets stop-band frequencies ranging from 3 to 300 GHz. The dataset is initially generated using full-wave electromagnetic simulations and augmented using a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) to enrich the feature space and scale the dataset to approximately one million samples. The cGAN is used exclusively during data preparation. Leveraging the augmented dataset, a GP-based surrogate model is constructed and expressed as a closed-form analytical relationship that maps the desired stop-band frequency and substrate material properties to the corresponding PRGW unit-cell dimensions. The proposed method improves reliability, significantly reduces computational time, and demonstrates superior performance compared with conventional trial-and-error procedures and traditional machine learning techniques in terms of mean squared error (MSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and efficiency. Experimental validation is performed through the design, fabrication, and measurement of two PRGW-based waveguides targeting Internet of Space (12-16 GHz) and mid-band 5 G (3-4 GHz) applications. The measured results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed GP-based surrogate modeling framework for automated RF and microwave component and subsystem design.

title: Are You Moving?

title: EffiReasonTrans: RL-Optimized Reasoning for Code Translation_supp1-3693795.pdf
authors: Mingwei Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Code translation is a crucial task in software development and maintenance. While recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have improved automated code translation accuracy, these gains often come at the cost of increased inference latency–hindering real-world development workflows that involve human-in-the-loop inspection. To address this tradeoff, we propose EffiReasonTrans, a training framework designed to improve translation accuracy while balancing inference latency. We first construct a high-quality reasoning-augmented dataset by prompting a stronger language model DeepSeek-R1 to generate intermediate reasoning and target translations. Each (source code, reasoning, target code) triplet undergoes automated syntax and functionality checks to ensure reliability. Based on this dataset, we employ a two-stage training strategy: supervised fine-tuning on reasoning-augmented samples, followed by reinforcement learning to further enhance accuracy, which also helps balance inference latency. We evaluate EffiReasonTrans on six translation pairs. Experimental results show that EffiReason-Trans consistently improves translation accuracy (up to +49.2% CA and +27.8% CodeBLEU compared to the base model), while reducing the number of generated tokens (up to -19.3%) and lowering inference latency in most cases (up to -29.0%). Ablation studies further confirm the complementary benefits of the two-stage training framework. Additionally, EffiReasonTrans shows improvements of translation accuracy when integrated into agent-based frameworks. Our code and data are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/DeepSoftwareAnalytics/EffiReasonTrans</uri>.

title: High-Precision SAR Imaging of Ship Target with 2D Spatial-Variant Phase Errors via Reversal Conjugate-Chirp Z-Transform and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
authors: JUNYAN LI, QING YANG, ZHONGYU LI, JUNJIE WU, JIANYU YANG
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) enables high-resolution imaging of ship target. However, the translational motion and intense three-dimensional (3D) oscillations of the target couple with the platform motion, introducing unknown two-dimensional (2D) spatial-variant (SV) phase errors into the echoes, causing severe defocusing of the target image. This challenges traditional SAR imaging methods for ship target, which can be summarized in the following two aspects: 1) traditional methods struggle to accurately estimate and compensate for phase errors with strong 2D SV characteristics, resulting in poor focusing performance, 2) traditional methods fail to fully utilize the SV signal structure, leading to low algorithmic efficiency. To address these issues, this paper proposes a high-precision SAR imaging method of ship target with 2D SV phase errors based on reversal conjugate-chirp z-transform and maximum likelihood estimation. The proposed method mainly consists of two steps: 1) the reversal conjugate-chirp z-transform is designed to adaptively extracting signals with varying azimuth centroids, thereby ensuring the precision of subsequent phase errors estimation, 2) the maximum likelihood estimation for 2D SV phase errors is derived, which not only enables highly efficient estimation under noisy conditions, approaching statistical optimality, but also offers better theoretical interpretability. Finally, high-resolution imaging of ship target is achieved through matrix-based phase errors compensation. Simulation and measured data validation show that the proposed method not only maintains the superior focusing performance but also achieves high computational efficiency.

title: Dynamic Event-Based Predefined-Time Adaptive Fuzzy Trajectory Tracking Control of Underwater Tracked Robot with Specified Accuracy
authors: Kelin Feng, Kewen Li, Yongming Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper investigates the robust adaptive predefined-time trajectory tracking control issue for underwater tracked robot with unknown track slipping and unknown dead-zone input. Fuzzy logic system (FLS) is employed to approximate the unknown nonlinear dynamic. A pair of parameter adaptive laws is designed to counteract the effects of unknown track slipping. To ensure that the trajectory tracking error converges to a specified accuracy within a predefined time, a predefined-time performance function is designed. Then, a barrier Lyapunov function (BLF) is constructed by embedding the performance function to avoid the singularity problem. In addition, a disturbance observer is constructed to observe and compensate for hydrodynamic disturbances present in the system dynamics, while a dynamic event-triggered mechanism is proposed to alleviate the computational load and reduce the actuator update frequency. Under the adaptive backstepping recursive design framework, a predefined-time event-triggered robust adaptive trajectory tracking control algorithm with performance constraints is proposed, which ensures system stability while enforcing predefined performance on the tracking error. Finally, the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed control scheme are validated through comparative simulations and data analysis.

title: A Heterogeneous System-on-Chip with an 83 GFLOp/s, 1.2 TFLOp/s/W Parallel Programmable Accelerator for Real-time On-device Learning in Autonomous Nano-UAVs
authors: Mattia Sinigaglia, Angelo Garofalo, Elia Cereda, Riccardo Tedeschi, Maicol Ciani, Huruy Tekle Tesfai, Mohammed Tolba, Victor Isachi, Hani Saleh, Daniele Palossi, Rafail Psiakis, Baker Mohammad, Davide Rossi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Solid-State Circuits Society
abstract: Miniaturized autonomous robotics, such as sub-10 cm nano-sized Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), is a novel and pervasive technology gaining attention in both civil and industrial application domains. To enable full autonomy, tiny robots must execute multiple real-time workloads, ranging from precise Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based perception algorithms to high-throughput control pipelines, while leveraging their minimal onboard computational resources. At the same time, well-trained AI-based algorithms suffer from the domain shift problem, which leads to poor performance when deployed in real-world domains. This work presents AlSaqr, a heterogeneous and secure RISC-V System-on-Chip (SoC) that integrates a dual-core Linux-capable 64-bit host processor, an 8-core 32-bit Programmable Parallel Accelerator (PPA) embedding an FP8/FP16 tensor core, and a secure subsystem. Fabricated in GlobalFoundries 22nm technology, the proposed Pixhawk FMUv6-compliant SoC operates from 210MHz at 0.52V to 900MHz at 0.8V within a power envelope from 90mW to 635mW occupying 9mm2 silicon footprint, matching the tight size, weight, and power constraints of nano-UAVs. Post-silicon results show that the PPA achieves a peak FP16 throughput of 83GFLOp/s at 900MHz and a peak energy efficiency of 1.2 TFLOp/s/W at 210MHz. To demonstrate the SoC’s capabilities, we showcase a deep learning model for human pose estimation that produces low-level setpoints for the nano-UAV’s control loops. AlSaqr achieves a 40 frame/s inference, while concurrently fine-tuning the same model with on-device learning (i.e., backpropagation), enabling real-time on-the-fly adaptation without interruption of the primary mission.

title: Hourly Precipitation Retrieval Using Fourier Neural Operator with GOES-16 and GOES-19 Inputs During the 4 July 2025 Texas Flash Flood_supp3-3693593.png

title: A Multibit ΔΣ TDC With Space–Time Averaging for UWB Transceivers Achieving ToF-Based Fine Ranging
authors: Minsong Zhang, Bowen Wang, Nannan Shi, Xin An, Rong Zhou, Zhangming Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: This work presents a multibit <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta \Sigma $</tex-math> </inline-formula> time-to-digital converter (TDC) employing a space–time averaging (STA) technique, designed for ultrawideband (UWB) transceivers to achieve fine-resolution time-of-flight (ToF) ranging. The proposed TDC offers both high time resolution and strong immunity to bit errors. By dynamically controlling the input reference signals of multiple phase detectors (PDs), the STA technique significantly reduces the quantization error of the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta \Sigma $</tex-math> </inline-formula> TDC, thereby achieving fine time resolution with a limited oversampling ratio (OSR). Additionally, by introducing a delay in the PD reset path, the TDC provides strong immunity to bit errors caused by UWB receiver misdetection. Implemented in 65-nm CMOS, the proposed multibit <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta \Sigma $</tex-math> </inline-formula> TDC achieves a root-mean-square (rms) error of 14.43ps with an OSR of 50, consuming <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$276.5~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>W at a 10-MHz clock frequency from a 1-V supply.

title: Radio-Frequency Interference Suppression Under Amplitude and Phase Distortions Via Spectral Flatness
authors: Bowen Cai, Xinliang Chen, Rui Zhu, Quanhua Liu
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Radar systems in practical environments are often affected by radio-frequency interference, which is commonly mitigated by spatial cancellation using auxiliary antennas. However, the use of auxiliary antennas inevitably introduces frequency-dependent amplitude and phase distortions between antennas, degrading signal synthesis and suppression performance. Existing calibration methods usually rely on prior measurements or explicit parameter estimation, making them sensitive to model mismatch, especially in multi-interference scenarios with spectral overlap. This letter proposes a spectral flatness measure (SFM)-based method for distortion compensation and interference suppression. The received signal is decomposed into subbands, where distortions are approximated as locally constant and compensated independently, and all parameters are jointly optimized via a genetic algorithm using the SFM criterion. The method requires no prior information or external calibration signals and achieves robust interference suppression. Simulation and experimental results validate its effectiveness in real interference environments.

title: Targeted Adversarial Attacks Against Deep Learning-Based Wideband Spectrum Sensing
authors: Shilian Zheng, Zhihao Ye, Luxin Zhang, Keqiang Yue, Zhijin Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Machine Learning in Communications and Networking
abstract: Deep learning-based wideband spectrum sensing models have achieved impressive performance in subband occupancy detection, but their susceptibility to adversarial attacks raises serious concerns. In this paper, we construct a targeted adversarial attack framework specifically designed for multi-label wideband spectrum sensing tasks. We further propose two optimization-based attack methods. MLS CW formulates perturbation generation as a constrained optimization problem and solves it via gradient descent. MLS DF is a greedy iterative method that approximates the solution to an underdetermined linear system through linearization. Both attacks are developed under strict power constraints and are capable of generating subtle yet highly effective perturbations. These attacks further incorporate a threshold-aware loss and selective subband targeting to enhance precision and stealthiness. Extensive experiments across various SIR conditions demonstrate that the proposed attacks significantly degrade the model’s detection performance in both untargeted and targeted scenarios, while maintaining high stealthiness. These results expose the severe vulnerability of DL-based spectrum sensing models and highlight the need for further research into their robustness under adversarial settings.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation in Spatio-Temporal Echocardiography Segmentation_supp2-3693615.mp4
authors: Arnaud Judge
publish: N/A
abstract: Domain adaptation methods aim to bridge the gap between datasets by enabling knowledge transfer across domains, reducing the need for additional expert annotations. However, many approaches struggle with reliability in the target domain, an issue particularly critical in medical image segmentation, where accuracy and anatomical validity are essential. This challenge is further exacerbated in spatio-temporal data, where the lack of temporal consistency can significantly degrade segmentation quality, and particularly in echocardiography, where the presence of artifacts and noise can further hinder segmentation performance. To address these issues, we present RL4Seg3D, an unsupervised domain adaptation framework for 2D + time echocardiography segmentation. RL4Seg3D integrates novel reward functions and a fusion scheme to enhance key landmark precision in its segmentations while processing full-sized input videos. By leveraging reinforcement learning for image segmentation, our approach improves accuracy, anatomical validity, and temporal consistency while also providing, as a beneficial side effect, a robust uncertainty estimator, which can be used at test time to further enhance segmentation performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework on over 30,000 echocardiographic videos, showing that it outperforms standard domain adaptation techniques without the need for any labels on the target domain. Code is available at https://github.com/arnaudjudge/RL4Seg3D.

title: IEEE VR 2026 Visualization and Graphics Technical Community (VGTC) Statement
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: The IEEE Visualization and Graphics Technical Community (VGTC) is a formal subcommittee of the Technical Activities Board (TAB) of the IEEE Computer Society. The VGTC provides technical leadership and organizes technical activities in the areas of visualization and visual analytics, computer graphics, virtual and augmented reality, and interaction.

title: MDDS-Net: Dual-Backbone Network With Dynamic Enhancement for SAR Ship Detection in Complex Maritime Environments
authors: Weimin Kong, Yun Zhang, Long Li, Niezipeng Kang, Qinglong Hua
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images is a key technology for maritime surveillance.However, practical applications face severe physical challenges, including low signal-to-clutter and signal-to-noise ratios under high sea states, discontinuous scattering of weak targets, and multi-target occlusion in dense ports. Existing methods struggle to suppress these severe interferences while preserving weak target structures. To address these issues, this paper proposes a detection framework based on a dual-backbone complementary coordination mechanism. The method employs a parallel-designed local and global encoder to extract fine-grained spatial details of ships and model long-range scene dependencies, respectively. To explicitly tackle the aforementioned physical degradations, two core modules are introduced. First, a geometric prior-guided multi-scale feature enhancement module (GPMS-FEM) integrates ship shape priors into spatial attention to aggregate discrete scattering centers, addressing weak target discontinuity and occlusion. Second, a bidirectional gated feature fusion module (BG-FFM) enables adaptive cross-backbone feature decoupling to suppress severe sea clutter in low SNR/SCR environments. Additionally, an adaptive tiling strategy guided by land-sea masks is employed to efficiently process large-scale SAR images. Experiments demonstrate that MDDS-Net achieves superior performance on the HRSID and RSDD-SAR datasets. Cross-validation using GF-3 satellite data and Automatic Identification System data further validates the robustness and practical value of our approach under complex maritime conditions.

title: Embodied Agent-Facilitated Virtual Support Groups for University Students
authors: Mina Kian, Yunwen Wang, Shriya Upadhyay, Zongjian Li, Abhyuday Singh, Yuanfeixue Nan, Mallory North, Kaitlin Zareno, Pau Sang, Wallace Browning, Jessica Wang, Lynn Carol Miller, Mohammad Soleymani, Maja Matarić
publish: IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing
abstract: Facilitator-guided support groups allow individuals facing similar challenges to meet and discuss their experiences. With the rising demand for mental health resources and therapist burnout, technology-facilitated support groups may offer a supplementary mechanism for supporting individuals while fostering peer support. Through a between- and within-subjects Wizard of-Oz study, we investigated how embodied conversational agent (ECA)-guided support groups using a robot embodiment and Large Language Model (LLM)-generated responses impact participant pre- to post-session 1) anxiety, 2) positive affect, and 3) negative affect, when participants perceive the ECA to be human-operated vs. when they are led to believe it is autonomous. Results showed significant decreases in participants' levels of anxiety and negative affect, and no significant change in positive affect before and after they participated in the support group. There were no statistically significant differences between the two conditions in these change patterns as well as participants' perceived trust and evaluation of the ECA's competence as a facilitator. Analysis of objective LIWC-22 (Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count) measures agreed with results from self-reported data: there were also no significant differences in the participants' expressed emotions in language between the two conditions. The implications of this study on ECA-facilitated support group interactions for mental health are discussed.

title: Analysis of radiator thermal cooling using machine learning systems
authors: D. Ramasam, Sivaraos, Dini Cahyandari, K. Kadirgama, M.M Rahman
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Preparation of a stable nanofluid is a concern this is by measuring the thermal conductivity and viscosity of the nanofluid. Inherently it is to develop equations for predictions from the measured values of these nanofluids for repetitive future testing. The measurable aspects include the selected nanoparticles, the base fluid and the particle volume concentration in the nanofluid. The nanoparticles selected for the project were Silicon Carbide nanoparticles with variable particle sizes ranging between 5-25 nm while the base fluid was a commercial coolant containing 50% Ethylene Glycol and 50% water. The results produced the nanofluid using a two-step method, separating the nanoparticles and their dispersion in the base fluid. They used a TEMPOS portable thermal analyzer to measure the nanofluid's thermal conductivity. For viscosity, they used a LVDV -III Ultra Rheometer (Viscometer). By using Artificial Neural Network theory the model indicated that the composition of nanoparticles increased the thermal conductivity of the solution overall. When the composition of volume concentration of Silicone Carbide increased thermal conductivity was in direct proportions. The varying concentration effected the viscosity and this in turn reduced the thermal conductivity. Overall, the artificial model was able to predict both parameters of thermal conductivity and viscosity.
