title: Optimal Two-Sided Pricing and Service Introduction Strategies for Competing Platforms
authors: Xiaoxu Chen, Xin Wen, Lipan Feng, Lei Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management
abstract: Nowadays, many two-sided platforms are actively launching new services beyond their main service to reach a broader range of consumers (e.g., A map adding local lifestyle services in addition to its navigation service). As users migrate across different services, the original two-sided pricing mechanisms are altered due to network effects. Furthermore, different service introduction strategies adopted by competing platforms also reshape the competitive landscape. In this paper, we develop a game-theoretical model to examine the optimal pricing strategies of two competing platforms when introducing new services. We theoretically show that when platforms introduce new services, they typically reduce consumer-side fees or even offer subsidies. However, on the provider side, whether platforms adjust their pricing strategies depends on the service introduction strategies of their competitors. Counter intuitively, the profitability of new services does not depend solely on their development costs; the intensity of market competition also leads platforms to adopt different equilibrium strategies. When competition is intense, platforms may fall into a “prisoner's dilemma” when introducing new services. In contrast, when competition is weak, introducing new services is more likely to result in a win-win outcome. Finally, we show that service complementarity increases the likelihood of a prisoner's dilemma but does not affect the win-win outcome. We further extend the analysis by endogenizing the user conversion rate and introducing asymmetric consumer base utility, and show that our main findings remain robust.

title: Contactless Electrical Impedance Tomography for Lung Ventilation Monitoring: Feasibility and Reconstruction Evaluation
authors: Yanyu Zheng, Yuxi Guo, Maomao Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Contactless Electrical Impedance Tomography (CEIT) enables non-contact measurement through capacitive coupling between electrodes and the subject, offering potential advantages for long-term lung ventilation monitoring. However, the reconstruction characteristics of CEIT for lung ventilation monitoring (LVM) and its adaptability to different reconstruction algorithms under experimental conditions remain insufficiently validated. This study presents a CEIT-based LVM method that uses impedance amplitude and phase-angle information for separate image reconstruction and conductivity-distribution evaluation, and establishes a synchronous testing system for CEIT and conventional Electrical Impedance Tomography (EIT). Water-tank experiments with cylindrical targets were conducted to evaluate four classic reconstruction algorithms for CEIT and conventional EIT, including Linear Back Projection (LBP), Tikhonov regularization, Newton-Raphson (N-R) iteration, and Landweber iteration, and to identify the more suitable reconstruction strategy for each system. Preliminary simulated lung imaging was then performed to assess the practical feasibility of CEIT. The results show that CEIT can achieve imaging performance comparable to that of conventional EIT under the reported experimental conditions, while exhibiting different reconstruction behaviors. Among the tested algorithms, Landweber iteration provided the most stable performance for CEIT, whereas Tikhonov regularization was more suitable for conventional EIT in this study. These findings support the feasibility of CEIT as a non-contact method for lung ventilation monitoring.

title: Multi-Modal Fusion for Underwater Localization through Hierarchical Uncertainty Awareness
authors: Yuanbo Xue, Yaming Ou, Junfeng Fan, Chao Zhou, Bing Wang, Chih-yung Wen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Traditional vehicle localization methods, such as visual simultaneous localization and mapping, often struggle underwater, especially under conditions like motion blur and low light. To address these challenges, this paper presents a novel multi-modal fusion system for underwater localization that integrates hierarchical uncertainty awareness (UA) into the acoustic/visual/inertial/pressure (AVIP) fusion framework, termed UA-AVIP, to achieve robust and accurate localization. The Doppler Velocity Log is introduced for reliable scale information and pressure sensor for water-depth measurements. A dual-framework approach is introduced to parallelize the fusion of acoustic and pressure data in an Extended Kalman Filter framework with the integration of visual and inertial measurements via a visual-inertial system. The final estimates are subsequently unified through factor graph optimization. Furthermore, a hierarchical UA strategy is developed to effectively manage uncertainties at multiple levels within the dual-framework fusion. At the measurement level, noise variance is adaptively estimated through spectrum analysis. At the module level, a deep learning-assisted sub-model enhances acoustic and pressure estimation. At the system level, an adaptive weighting strategy within the factor graph addresses dynamic uncertainties. Both self-collected and open-source datasets in the pool, lakes, and oceans under distinct scenarios are used to train and validate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed UA-AVIP system. The results underscore the critical importance of UA in underwater localization. The source code is available at https://github.com/HKPolyU-UAV/UA-AVIP.

title: Enabling ISAC with Simultaneous Transmission and Reflection-Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces
authors: Liming Wang, Nan Wu, Rongkun Jiang, Haoyang Li, Weijie Yuan, Arumugam Nallanathan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Multi-layer stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIM) have shown considerable potential for wave-domain beamforming in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems. However, conventional SIM is confined to half-space coverage imposed by transmission-only components. While simultaneous transmission and reflection reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (STAR-RIS) overcome this via full-space beamforming, the single-layer architecture inherently limits the sensing and communication capabilities. Therefore, we propose a novel STAR-SIM architecture that embeds a SIM with a STAR surface for full-space beamforming, and formulate the joint optimization problem to maximize the sum rate subject to the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) constraint. To address the challenges of intractable sensing constraints and unique asymmetric multi-layer structure of STAR-SIM, we establish an intuitive link between the CRB and beampattern gain and facilitate an alternating optimization framework, where the STAR-SIM phase shifts are optimized by penalty-based gradient ascent (GA) and projected GA (PGA) algorithms with high efficiency and low complexity compared with conventional semidefinite relaxation (SDR) methods. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed STAR-SIM structure with full-space beamforming capability and showcase its transformative potential for next-generation ISAC networks.

title: Dynamics-Aware Gaussian Splatting Streaming Towards Fast On-the-Fly 4D Reconstruction_supp1-3688730.mp4
authors: Zehong Lin
publish: N/A
abstract: The recent development of 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) has led to great interest in 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction. Existing approaches mainly rely on full-length multi-view videos, while there has been limited exploration of online reconstruction methods that enable on-the-fly training and per-timestep streaming. Current 3DGS-based streaming methods treat the Gaussian primitives uniformly and constantly renew the densified Gaussians. Thus, they overlook the difference between dynamic and static features and neglect the temporal continuity of the scene. To address these limitations, we propose a novel pipeline for iterative streamable 4D dynamic spatial reconstruction. It comprises three stages: a selective inheritance stage that retains priors from previous timesteps to preserve the temporal continuity, a dynamics-aware shift stage that distinguishes dynamic and static primitives and employs distinct strategies to optimize their movements, and an error-guided densification stage that efficiently identifies Gaussians requiring densification to accommodate emerging objects. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in online 4D reconstruction, demonstrating compact storage, the fastest on-the-fly training, and superior representation quality.

title: Grand Challenges in Cross Reality
authors: Christoph Anthes, Mark Billinghurst, Uwe Grünefeld, Hans-Christian Jetter, Hai-Ning Liang, Frank Maurer, David Aigner, Craig Anslow, Guillaume Bataille, Abraham G. Campbell, Judith Friedl-Knirsch, Alexander Gall, Renan Guarese, Sebastian Hubenschmid, Yue Li, Fabian Pointecker, Andreas Riegler, Daniel Roth, Rishi Vanukuru, Nanjia Wang, Lingyun Yu, Johannes Zagermann, Daniel Zielasko
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Cross Reality (CR) is a new emerging field based on the current developments in Mixed Reality hardware, especially supported by the broad market penetration of video-based see-through Head-Mounted Displays. It refers to applications that span across different stages (real, Augmented Reality, Augmented Virtuality, Virtual Reality) of the reality-virtuality continuum, where users are interconnected between different stages and/or are able to transition between these stages. This publication follows the concept of other grand challenges publications and reflects the discussion of various researchers invested in CR. After an initial discussion at the 1<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">st</sup> Joint Workshop on Cross Reality at IEEE ISMAR 2023, six topic groups have been identified, leading to 22 challenges, which were discussed in groups over the period of multiple months. The discussion of these challenges should act as a road map for future research in the area of CR.

title: Handle-based Mesh Deformation Guided By Vision Language Model
authors: Xingpeng Sun, Shiyang jia, Zherong Pan, Kui Wu, Aniket Bera
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Mesh deformation is a fundamental tool in 3D content manipulation. Despite extensive prior research, existing approaches often suffer from low output quality, require significant manual tuning, or depend on data-intensive training. To address these limitations, we introduce a training-free, handle-based mesh deformation method. Our core idea is to leverage a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to interpret and manipulate a handle-based interface. We begin by applying cone singularity detection to identify a sparse set of potential handles. The VLM is then prompted to select both the deformable sub-parts of the mesh and the handles that best align with user instructions. Subsequently, we query the desired deformed positions of the selected handles in screen space. To reduce uncertainty inherent in VLM predictions, we aggregate the results from multiple camera views using a novel multi-view voting scheme. Across a suite of benchmarks, our method produces deformations that align more closely with user intent, as measured by CLIP and GPTEval3D scores, while introducing low distortion—quantified via membrane energy. In summary, our approach is highly automated and consistently delivers high-quality mesh deformations.

title: Avian-Scale Flapping-Wing Air Vehicle With Independent Actuation Mechanism for Enhanced Lateral Maneuverability_supp2-3688743.mp4
authors: Jae-Hung Han
publish: N/A
abstract: Birds achieve remarkable aerial maneuverability through bilateral independent control of their wings, enabling agile flight in confined spaces and at low speeds. However, implementing such independent actuation in medium-to-heavy (>400 g) avian-scale flapping-wing air vehicles (FWAVs) has remained challenging due to power limitations of servo-driven approaches or unsynchronized phases in dual motor-linkage approaches. This article presents an independent actuation mechanism with encoder-based synchronization and validates its application to heavy avian-scale FWAVs through the 940 g KRoHawk-I platform. Each wing is driven by a separate actuation unit comprising a BLDC motor, gear train, and linkage mechanism. The actuation units are capable of rotation about the pitch axis to enable asymmetric stroke plane control. The synchronization controller maintains crank angle errors within 10° for stable flight, validated through ground tests. For lateral maneuvering, asymmetric stroke plane control generates differential angles of attack and trajectories between main wings, producing rolling moments nine times as large as those generated by tail control. Flight tests demonstrate significant maneuverability enhancement compared to tail control with symmetric stroke plane: 65% reduction in distance required for 90° turns and 70% reduction in roll response time.

title: Holistic Energy Performance Management: Enablers, Capabilities, and Features
authors: Meysam Masoudi, Milad Ganjalizadeh, Tahar Zanouda, Pal Frenger
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: Energy consumption is a significant concern for mobile network operators, and to enable further network energy improvements it is also an important target when developing the emerging 6G standard. In this paper we show that, despite the existence of many energy-saving features in 5G new radio (NR) networks, activating them in isolation yields only suboptimal savings and often compromises other network key performance indicators (KPIs) such as coverage or latency. We first introduce a compact taxonomy that distinguishes hardware capabilities from higher-layer features. Features fall into two classes: (i) signaling and scheduling mechanisms that create idle windows, and (ii) features that utilize those windows to save energy. We then present a feature orchestrator as a logical node to coordinate between features to maximize the gain. Using a 3GPP-aligned simulator with product-realistic parameters, we show that coordinating lean NR, scheduling, and advanced sleep modes significantly reduces gNodeB (gNB) energy consumption with negligible throughput loss, compared to the uncoordinated scenario. We conclude by outlining open issues in observability, system dynamics, coordination, and intelligent automation for energy performance management.

title: RIVDCB-UNet: a robust vessel detection in cluttered backgrounds via UNet for SDGSAT-1 thermal infrared data
authors: Kun Li, Tao Wang, Hongzhao Tang, Zhongchang Sun, Dailiang Peng, Yonggang Qian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Aiming at complex and variable scenarios with cloud and fog obscuration and diverse interference sources, a robust infrared vessel detection in cluttered backgrounds via the UNet (RIVDCB-UNet) is designed. This model effectively overcomes the limitations of traditional UNet in small target detection and significantly improves its detection performance. The RIVDCB-UNet model takes a single thermal infrared image as input and incorporates the BCE-Dice loss function to ensure stability and accuracy during the training process. The model integrates the EfficientNet backbone network for efficient feature extraction, followed by feature upsampling and fusion through a U-shaped decoder. Finally, an eight-connected neighborhood clustering module is introduced to further refine pixel-level classification results and achieve precise localization. The training of the proposed model leverages the publicly available NUDT-SIRST-Sea Dataset and our self-constructed SDGSAT-1 thermal infrared vessel detection dataset. Data augmentation techniques, including rotation and cropping, are employed to expand the dataset, thereby addressing the issue of target-background imbalance in the original datasets. This imbalance can cause the model to overly focus on information-sparse background regions. The proposed model achieves detection rates, recall rates, and F1 scores of 95.39%, 97.89%, 96.62% on the NUDT-SIRST-Sea Dataset, and 97.81%, 96.78%, 97.29% on the SDGSAT-1 dataset, respectively. These results are significantly higher than those of other models such as ACM (84.81%, 73.17%, 78.56% on NUDT-SIRST-Sea and 90.17%, 72.87%, 80.60% on SDGSAT-1) and DNANet (89.98%, 83.10%, 86.40% on NUDT-SIRST-Sea and 95.64%, 88.21%, 91.77% on SDGSAT-1).

title: Multi-Scale Spectral-Spatial Coordinated Awareness Fusion Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Haizhu Pan, Ruyi Yao, Bopeng Ren, Haimiao Ge, Cuiping Shi, Xiaoqian Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate hyperspectral image (HSI) classification remains challenging, as discriminative performance critically depends on the effective coordination of high-dimensional spectral information and complex spatial structures. Recent deep learning methods, particularly attention mechanisms, have improved HSI classification. However, existing models still face spectral over-smoothing, spatial ambiguity, and poor spectral-spatial coordination. To address these limitations, a multi-scale spectral-spatial coordinated awareness fusion network (MS<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>CAFNet) is proposed for HSI classification. The proposed framework adopts a dual-branch architecture to explicitly decouple spectral and spatial feature learning, thereby alleviating cross-domain interference. In the spectral branch, a difference-gated multi-scale spectral attention (DGMSA) module is designed to jointly model spectral similarity and difference cues under multiple receptive fields, enabling an adaptive balance between redundancy suppression and fine-grained discrimination. In the spatial branch, a multi-head coordinate-aware spatial attention (MHCASA) module is introduced, which integrates explicit positional encoding with long-range contextual modeling to enhance robustness in spatially heterogeneous scenes. Furthermore, a bidirectional cross-guided fusion (BCGF) strategy is developed to align and coordinate spectral and spatial representations through correlation-based interaction, ensuring semantic correspondence across branches. Experimental results on four publicly available HSI datasets demonstrate that the network outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: Perfect Quantum Teleportation in Memory Amplitude-Damping Channels Based on Pre-flipping and Environment-Assisted Measurement
authors: Shiye Zhang, Sajede Harraz, Sen Kuang, Shuang Cong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Quantum Engineering
abstract: Most existing quantum teleportation schemes do not consider the memory effect of noise, which is becoming increasingly serious in practical quantum communication. In this paper, we propose two quantum teleportation protocols for noisy memory channels: one based on pre-flipping (PF) and another incorporating pre-flipping with environment-assisted measurement (PF-EAM). In the PF protocol, a pre-flipping operation is applied to the entangled qubits before entanglement distribution to enhance robustness against noise. A recovery operator is then used to reverse the pre-flipping operation. In the PF-EAM protocol, environment-assisted measurement (EAM) is added to the entanglement distribution process, further improving teleportation fidelity. Since the pre-flipping operator in the two protocols is a fixed unitary operator, our protocols exclude additional parameters, which is unlike weak measurement (WM) schemes that rely on adjustable measurement strength. We derive the analytical expressions for the average fidelity and the success probability of our teleportation protocols. The results show that the pre-flipping operator in the PF protocol effectively enhances the average fidelity of quantum teleportation in a deterministic manner, while the fidelity of PF-EAM remains at constant 1, unaffected by the noise intensity in the channel. In addition, our PF-EAM achieves a higher success probability while maintaining the fidelity of 1 with respect to the existing unprotected teleportation protocol, protocol based on weak measurement and measurement reversal (WM-MR), and protocol based on quantum feed-forward control and environment-assisted measurement (QFFC-EAM) framework.

title: Collaborative Signal Fusion Network for Crowd Counting in Smart City Systems
authors: Kun Chen, Shengwei Jia, Cheng Xu, Junbin Su, Shihui Zhang
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Crowd counting plays an important role in smart cities, but existing crowd counting methods still face pain points such as insufficient modal fusion, inadequate scale perception, and high model complexity. In view of these, we draw inspiration from the idea of collaborative reasoning and propose a collaborative signal fusion network (CSFNet) for crowd counting. The network consists of two core modules: the modal collaborative reasoning module and the scale collaborative reasoning module, which perform cross-modal information complementarity and multi-scale density perception, respectively. Among them, the modal collaborative reasoning module fully utilizes the cross-modal interactive fusion mechanism, significantly enhancing the adaptability of CSFNet in extreme environments such as darkness, fog, or smog. The scale collaborative reasoning module integrates multi-scale features and explores the crowd distribution information in local and global features, greatly enhancing the network's ability to perceive crowd density in scenes with uneven distribution. To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first research to deeply couple modal collaborative reasoning and scale collaborative reasoning into an end-to-end network for RGB-T crowd counting based on density map regression, addressing the dual challenges of insufficient modal fusion in extreme lighting environments and inadequate scale perception in uneven density scenes. The experimental results show that CSFNet achieves competitive performance and state-of-the-art complexity, providing a possibility for the deployment of edge devices in smart city systems. We will release the code at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/KunChenKKK/CSFNet</uri>.

title: Fuzzy Boundary Control of Spatial 2-D Nonlinear Distributed Parameter Systems Under Mobile Sensors
authors: Xiao-Wei Zhang, Xiao-Qiong Li, Xiaoli Li, Huai-Ning Wu, Zi-Peng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper investigates observer-based fuzzy boundary control problem for spatial two-dimensional (2-D) nonlinear distributed parameter systems modeled by parabolic partial differential equations (PDEs) with mobile sensors. Firstly, considering the complexity of systems, a Takagi-Sugeno (T-S) fuzzy model is adopted to precisely represent the nonlinear term of the PDE system. Subsequently, a Luenberger-type fuzzy observer is proposed to estimate the state of the PDE system, and a mobile sensor guidance scheme is introduced to restrict the sensors to move within the designated regions, thereby effectively avoiding collisions among them. In the proposed mobile sensor strategy, the spatial domain is divided into several sub-domains based on the number of sensors. Then, employing the Lyapunov method, a fuzzy boundary control method based on the fuzzy observer is developed to ensure the asymptotic stability of closed-loop fuzzy system. Finally, two simulation examples are provided to demonstrate the validity of the proposed approach.

title: WINning Against Audio Deepfakes
authors: Arth J. Shah, Aniket Pandey, Hemant A. Patil
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: In this work, we propose the Wavelet Integrated Network (WIN), a novel neural architecture and employ it for Audio Deepfake Detection (ADD). Unlike standard transformer-based models that mix information across tokens via global self-attention, WIN replaces these attention blocks with intra-token wavelet atoms to enhance intra-token understanding of the network. These atoms perform localized time-frequency analysis inside each token, capturing spectral patterns that emphasize local structure rather than the global context. We further propose Multi-Head Wavelet (MHW), and demonstrate that the proposed network has a lower number of parameters as compared to the existing baseline systems. In addition, we conduct ablation studies on various real and analytic wavelets to further optimize the network. We compared our work with baseline, and demonstrate $\approx$ 2 % decrease in pooled EER over 9 competitive datasets for ADD research. All the codes are publicly available at https://github.com/Arth-Shah/WIN.

title: Enhanced High-Temperature Multi-Parameter Sensor With Cascaded FBG and Open Fabry-Pérot Probe
authors: Jiandong Bai, Yujie Han, Haoyan Chai, Yongqiu Zheng, Chenyang Xue
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Traditional sensing technologies usually can only measure a single physical parameter and lack temperature compensation functionality, making it difficult to meet the urgent demands for multi-parameter synchronization and precise monitoring. In order to address this issue, we present an enhanced high-temperature broadband noise-acceleration in-situ fiber-optic composite sensor based on cascaded fiber Bragg grating (FBG) and Fabry-Pérot (F-P) interferometer probe. The sensitive core structure prepared by arc discharge technology is composed of two sets of FBGs, multimode fiber (MMF), hollow-core fiber (HCF), and photonic crystal fiber (PCF). Two sets of FBGs written by femtosecond laser are placed on a sensitive diaphragm with a mass block and a packaging surface to achieve acceleration and temperature monitoring, respectively. An open-type F-P cavity formed by HCF and PCF is used to enhance noise response. Besides, FBG placed on the packaging surface is also used to achieve temperature compensation to prevent the influence of temperature on the other two parameters. Experimental results show that the self-designed sensor can perform self-calibration within the range of 25-600 °C. And it has a maximum sensitivity of 0.082 mV/Pa in the range of 100 Hz-10 kHz for noise signals. It can also simultaneously conduct acceleration tests over a wide range of 0-1938 g, and has a large bandwidth of 10-2100 Hz for acceleration signals. Furthermore, the sensor exhibits excellent consistency, repeatability, and stability, which can be applied to long-term in-situ multi-parameter health monitoring and fault diagnosis in extremely harsh environments such as aviation engines and gas turbines.

title: IEEE VR 2026 Paper Reviewers
